    "description": "## Problem\n\nT20260423-0506 trims graph read tools toward summary-first responses (e.g. `orbit.graph.pack` defaults to signatures with `summary: false` as an opt-in for full bodies). That's the right intermediate step for v2 — it keeps the body path available for agents that genuinely need it.\n\nBut the v1 evidence (`benchmarks/graph_v1/ISSUES.md` §Concrete Issues #4 and §\"Duplicate raw file verification after graph already answered the question\") suggests a stronger design: **graph tools should never return source text at all.** Agents that need bodies can chain a targeted `cat` / `Read`; the graph's job is to return *coordinates*, not *payload*. This aligns with how LLMs are trained — far more `cat → edit` examples than `orbit.graph.pack → orbit.graph.write`.\n\nGoing one step further, every pointer should include a **selector** string — the canonical handoff to `orbit.graph.write` / `orbit.graph.add` / `orbit.graph.delete` / `orbit.graph.move`. Without it, any agent that decides to use structural writes has to reconstruct the selector from `{file, symbol, kind}`, which is fragile. With it, the read→write chain is copy-complete.\n\n## Why \"v3 candidate\" and not v2\n\nv2 is already running T-0506's softer \"summary default\" shape. We don't want to change the design under the benchmark mid-stream — v2's whole purpose is measuring the v2-era surface. Once v2 data lands, we will know:\n\n- Whether \"summary default + body opt-in\" moved hybrid utilization meaningfully.\n- Whether agents ever exercised the body opt-in path.\n- Whether `orbit.graph.write` saw any use alongside the trimmed read surface.\n\nIf v2 shows agents never opted into bodies: strong evidence that this harder spec is correct — strip bodies entirely.\nIf v2 shows agents relied on bodies: we learn exactly which tool / task class, and scope the exception.\n\nEither way, v2's data informs this task's AC. Hence: **blocked on v2 sweep completion**, not started earlier.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nEvery token of body text returned by a graph tool is paid on every agent turn that references that message. v1 measured this directly: `orbit.graph.pack` returning full impl bodies on `locate-agentruntime` contributed ~4,500 chars / ~1,127 tokens per call, and the agent *then re-read the same files via sed* to verify. The body was dead weight.\n\nBeyond cost, the pointer-only discipline pushes graph tools toward a sharper mental model: **graph answers structural questions, filesystem answers content questions**. That clarity helps agents pick the right tool and helps future tool authors avoid re-inventing `cat`.\n\nThe mandatory `selector` field is the load-bearing corollary: if the graph is going to stop shipping bodies, it must make the read→write handoff frictionless, or `orbit.graph.write` will lose ground to `fs.write` even for operations where structural correctness matters.\n\n## Proposal\n\n### Part A — strip all source text from graph read tool responses\n\nAffected tools: `orbit.graph.pack`, `orbit.graph.show`, `orbit.graph.refs`, `orbit.graph.implementors`, `orbit.graph.callers`, `orbit.graph.deps`, `orbit.graph.search`.\n\n- No multi-line source blocks in any response.\n- `signature` lines are allowed (one-line type / function signatures — structural metadata, not source body).\n- Doc comments are **not** returned.\n- `orbit.graph.pack` loses its `summary` parameter entirely — there is no \"full body\" mode.\n- `orbit.graph.overview` is explicitly out of scope (it's a structural summary, not a pointer tool; T-0506 handles it separately).\n\n### Part B — every pointer entry carries a selector string\n\nAll pointer-returning tools emit entries matching this shape (field names indicative, tune during implementation):\n\n```json\n{\n  \"symbol\": \"AgentRuntime\",\n  \"kind\": \"trait\",\n  \"file\": \"crates/orbit-agent/src/runtime/runtime_trait.rs\",\n  \"line_start\": 45,\n  \"line_end\": 58,\n  \"signature\": \"pub trait AgentRuntime: Send + Sync\",\n  \"selector\": \"symbol:crates/orbit-agent/src/runtime/runtime_trait.rs#AgentRuntime:trait\"\n}\n```\n\nThe `selector` string must:\n\n- Be a valid input to every `orbit.graph.write` / `add` / `delete` / `move` / `show` / `refs` tool that accepts selector input (i.e. round-trip correctly through the write tools' input parser).\n- Be suitable for direct copy-paste by the agent without reconstruction.\n- Match the exact format currently accepted by selector-consuming tools (no new selector dialect).\n\n`orbit.graph.search`'s existing `format: \"selectors\"` mode is NOT the same thing — that emits a flat list of selector strings with no other metadata. This task adds `selector` as a **field alongside** `{symbol, kind, file, ...}` in the structured response, so the agent gets the pointer and the handoff in one payload.\n\n## Decision dependencies on v2\n\nDo not start this task until **after** the v2 sweep lands and RESULTS are written. The v2 data informs:\n\n1. Whether any tool needs to retain a body-opt-in exception (Part A).\n2. What the canonical pointer schema should look like in detail — some fields listed above may be redundant after v2 usage patterns show which are load-bearing.\n3. Whether this task ships independently or is rolled into the T-0506 successor.\n\nIf v2 shows `summary: false` was never invoked: Part A ships unchanged.\nIf v2 shows graph-write was never invoked: Part B scope shrinks (no need to optimize for a dead handoff) — revisit the structural-write surface under the separate \"decide fate of graph-write\" ADR.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- This task depends on T-0506 having landed. Schema-size baseline and trimmed defaults are preconditions.\n- No new cross-crate deps. Touches `orbit-tools` (tool schemas + response shaping) and possibly thin helpers in `orbit-knowledge` (selector emission, if not already centralized).\n- Do not alter selector semantics. If reviewers find the existing selector grammar awkward, that's a separate task.\n- Update `crates/orbit-core/assets/skills/orbit-graph/SKILL.md` to show the pointer-only responses and the read→write chain using the new `selector` field.\n- The `overview` tool is out of scope unless it currently returns source text (it doesn't, as of v1).\n- This is a **v3-preparation** task. It should not be merged into the v2 sweep timeline; a PR against `main` is fine but the expected measurement window is the v3 sweep.\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Deleting or renaming the graph write tools (`write`, `add`, `delete`, `move`) — that decision waits on the separate ADR task, to be filed if v2 data warrants it.\n- Adding new graph tools.\n- Redesigning the selector grammar.\n- Running the v3 sweep. Sweep execution is a follow-up task once v3 fixtures are in place.\n- Modifying `benchmarks/graph_v1/**` or `benchmarks/graph_v2/**` once frozen.",

    "plan": "# Plan — Pointer-only graph reads + selector field\n\n> **Blocked on:** v2 graph sweep completion (`benchmarks/graph_v2/RESULTS.md` committed). Do not begin this task until v2 data is in.\n\n## Pre-flight (reviewer confirms before picking up)\n\n1. v2 sweep has landed. `benchmarks/graph_v2/RESULTS.md` exists.\n2. Re-read v2 RESULTS §Tool-utilization and §Cost tables. Specifically check:\n   - Did `orbit.graph.pack` with `summary: false` ever get called? If zero, Part A ships unchanged. If non-zero, record the task classes that used it; those become exception candidates.\n   - Did any graph-write tool (`write`, `add`, `delete`, `move`) get called? If zero, Part B's urgency drops — file the \"decide fate of graph-write\" ADR instead and hold this task.\n   - What's the hybrid utilization rate post-T-0506? If it moved meaningfully (say > 10%), the pointer-only discipline has measurable room to move it further; if not, there's a deeper problem upstream of tool shape and this task may need re-scoping.\n3. Write a one-paragraph preamble in the PR description recording the v2 signals and how they shape the AC scope.\n\n## Step 1 — Baseline schema-size measurement (post-T-0506)\n\n- Use the schema-size harness added in T20260423-0506 Step 1 (`crates/orbit-tools/tests/graph_schema_size.rs`).\n- Record the current `MAX_BYTES` as the post-T-0506 baseline. This task's reduction delta is measured against this number.\n\n## Step 2 — Selector emission audit\n\nBefore stripping bodies, ensure selectors are centrally producible.\n\n- Locate the selector-generation code in `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/selector.rs` (or equivalent). Confirm there is a single function that takes a graph node and returns its canonical selector string.\n- If such a function does not exist, add one in this task's first commit. All downstream shape changes depend on it.\n- Unit test: round-trip selectors through the existing parser for each `kind` variant (trait, struct, impl, function, method, field, module) using fixture nodes.\n\n## Step 3 — Strip bodies from read tools\n\nOrder: simplest first, so regressions show up early.\n\n### 3a. `orbit.graph.show`\n\n- Audit current response. If it returns body text, strip it.\n- Response shape per entry: `{symbol, kind, file, line_start, line_end, signature, selector}`.\n- Unit test asserts no body text in response for a fixture impl block.\n\n### 3b. `orbit.graph.implementors`, `orbit.graph.callers`, `orbit.graph.deps`\n\n- Each should already be pointer-ish. Verify and add `selector` field to every entry.\n- Unit tests assert `selector` present and body absent.\n\n### 3c. `orbit.graph.refs`\n\n- Partitioned response from T-0506 Step 3 is preserved. Each ref entry in `code_refs` / `doc_refs` / `config_refs` gets a `selector` field.\n- Unit test asserts selector present across all three partitions.\n\n### 3d. `orbit.graph.search`\n\n- Each result entry gets `selector` field. The existing `format: \"selectors\"` mode stays (emits flat list); the default structured mode adds `selector` alongside `{name, kind, file, ...}`.\n- Unit test asserts selector present in structured-mode output AND that both modes remain available.\n\n### 3e. `orbit.graph.pack`\n\n- Hardest change. This tool's current value prop was \"return bodies.\" Strip it.\n- Remove `summary` parameter from the schema.\n- Response shape per entry: same structured pointer as above.\n- Add a deprecation note in the tool description or a migration comment: \"Use Read / cat for bodies; this tool returns structural pointers only.\"\n- Unit test asserts `summary` param is rejected and no body text appears in response.\n\n## Step 4 — Skill doc update\n\nEdit `crates/orbit-core/assets/skills/orbit-graph/SKILL.md`:\n\n- Add a prominent \"Reading bodies\" section explaining the pointer-only contract: use `Read` (Claude) or `cat` (Codex) to inspect source; graph tools answer structural questions.\n- Add two end-to-end examples:\n  - `graph.implementors → Read line_start..line_end → fs.write`  (lexical edit path)\n  - `graph.implementors → Read line_start..line_end → graph.write selector=...`  (structural edit path using the selector handoff)\n- Remove or rewrite any example that assumed `pack --summary: false` returned bodies.\n\n## Step 5 — Schema-size re-measure + final checks\n\n- Re-run schema-size harness. Record post-change bytes. Reduction vs. Step 1 baseline goes in PR description.\n- `make build && make fmt && cargo test -p orbit-tools -p orbit-knowledge`.\n- Smoke test: run `orbit tool run orbit.graph.implementors --input '{\"trait_selector\":\"symbol:crates/orbit-agent/src/runtime/runtime_trait.rs#AgentRuntime:trait\"}'` against the live workspace; confirm response includes `selector` field on each entry.\n\n## Risks and mitigations\n\n- **Some consumer silently depended on body output.** Mitigation: grep for `orbit.graph.pack` invocations across `.md`, `.yaml`, `.rs` in the repo before Step 3e. Any invocation that expects bodies needs to be migrated to `Read`.\n- **Selector emission isn't centralized.** Mitigation: Step 2 addresses this as a first-class prerequisite, not an afterthought.\n- **v2 data points away from stripping bodies entirely.** Mitigation: the pre-flight gate in this plan explicitly reads v2 first. If v2 shows bodies were used, the implementing agent adjusts AC#2 to carve out the exception rather than blindly executing the original spec.\n\n## Out-of-scope checkpoints\n\n- Do not modify the graph write tools (`write`, `add`, `delete`, `move`). They become more relevant under this task but their fate is a separate ADR.\n- Do not change selector grammar.\n- Do not run v3 sweep in this task.\n- Do not touch `benchmarks/graph_v1/` or `benchmarks/graph_v2/` (if frozen).",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nThe v1 graph benchmark (`benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md`) found 1.7% graph-tool utilization across 60 hybrid runs. The one successful hybrid graph call — `orbit_graph_implementors` on the `AgentRuntime` trait — worked because that tool's name makes its purpose unambiguous. Every other graph tool was silently passed over in favor of grep.\n\nReading the current `ToolSchema.description` strings in `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_*.rs` suggests why: they are present-tense feature lists, not \"when to use\" guidance. Example (`orbit.graph.overview`):\n\n> \"Return an aggregate overview of the knowledge graph. Broad queries auto-compact above 50 files...\"\n\nThat tells an agent *what* the tool does, not *when it's the right tool*. An agent weighing `overview` vs. `rg` has no signal that one is better for structural maps and the other for lexical hits. Under uncertainty, agents default to the familiar surface — `rg`.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nThis is the single lowest-risk lever in the graph-surface improvement backlog. It is a pure-text change across 8 tool descriptions, with zero behavioral logic changes. That means:\n\n1. It can ship **ahead of** the broader tool-surface trim (T20260423-0506), decoupling description wording as an independent variable.\n2. A descriptive-text-only change provides an isolated signal: if v2 utilization rises on this change alone, we know wording was load-bearing. If not, the problem is deeper (return-payload shape, MCP schema tax, etc.) and T20260423-0506 is the right next intervention.\n\n## Relationship to T20260423-0506\n\nThis task **supersedes Step 2 of T20260423-0506**. When the latter is picked up for execution, Step 2 should be dropped from its plan and AC#5 should be removed from its acceptance criteria (or pointed at this task's completion as a precondition). The remaining T-0506 scope — `search` ranking, `refs` partitioning, `pack` summary default, `overview` default shape, schema-size reduction — stays intact.\n\n## Proposal\n\nRewrite the `ToolSchema.description` string on each of the 8 `orbit.graph.*` tools plus each tool's `ToolParam.description` strings. Every tool description must:\n\n1. Begin with the literal prefix `Use when `.\n2. Contain at least one sentence beginning with `Prefer over grep when ` (or `Use instead of grep when `) that names a specific grep-failure mode.\n3. Drop decorative framing (\"aggregate overview of the knowledge graph\", \"Search knowledge graph nodes by name or location\"). Every token is paid on every turn via the MCP schema.\n\nSuggested description stems (implementing agent should refine):\n\n- `orbit.graph.search` — \"Use when you need to locate a typed symbol (trait / struct / function). Prefer over grep when name collisions across crates would make lexical search ambiguous.\"\n- `orbit.graph.overview` — \"Use when you need a structural map of a directory's symbols. Prefer over grep when you want counts and shape, not lexical hits.\"\n- `orbit.graph.refs` — \"Use when you need every reference to a symbol. Prefer over grep when you need to distinguish construct-sites from pattern-matches, or separate code refs from doc/config mentions.\"\n- `orbit.graph.pack` — \"Use when you know a target symbol and want its definition + immediate context. Prefer over grep when lexical search would also return pattern-matches or string literals containing the name.\"\n- `orbit.graph.show` — \"Use when you want one symbol's definition with its neighbors. Prefer over grep when you need structural context like children or impl relationships.\"\n- `orbit.graph.implementors` — \"Use when you need every type that implements a trait. Prefer over grep when the trait has generic or re-exported impls that `impl TraitName` misses.\"\n- `orbit.graph.callers` — \"Use when you need to walk callers transitively. Prefer over grep — grep only does one hop and loses context across function boundaries.\"\n- `orbit.graph.deps` — \"Use when you need crate-level dependency direction. Prefer over grep when `path =` vs `version =` vs dev-dependencies matter.\"\n\nParameter descriptions follow the same discipline: trim decoration, keep the information agents need to pick a value.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Pure text change. No logic changes in `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_*.rs` beyond the `description` string literals.\n- Update `crates/orbit-core/assets/skills/orbit-graph/SKILL.md` if any description-referenced behavior changes — it should not, but sanity-check.\n- Do NOT touch `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/**`. Service-layer logic is out of scope.\n- Ship this as its own commit with a clear message (\"graph-tools: rewrite descriptions for when-to-use clarity\") so its effect can be bisected later if needed.",
    "execution_summary": "Rewrote the schema text for all 12 registered `orbit.graph.*` tools so every top-level description starts with `Use when ` and includes a grep-comparison sentence; the task body named 8 read tools, but the acceptance test is phrased against every `schema().name.starts_with(\"orbit.graph.\")` entry, so the four graph mutators were updated too. Added `crates/orbit-tools/tests/graph_tool_descriptions.rs` to enforce that registry contract. After review feedback, revised the read-side descriptions for `orbit.graph.implementors`, `orbit.graph.callers`, and `orbit.graph.pack` to use a hybrid heuristic-plus-behavior style so the schema still exposes non-obvious matching and output semantics. Updated `crates/orbit-core/assets/skills/orbit-graph/SKILL.md` to route relationship questions to `implementors` / `callers` / `refs` / `deps` correctly and to note that `orbit.graph.pack` `file:` selectors do not return full source. Verification: `cargo test -p orbit-tools`, `make fmt`, and `make build` passed. Committed as `5f861caf` (`[T20260423-0525] graph-tools: rewrite descriptions for when-to-use clarity`) and fast-forward merged into `agent-main`.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nThe v1 graph benchmark headline finding — \"agents invoked graph tools in 1 of 60 hybrid runs\" — was produced by an ad-hoc transcript-level Python scan at report-generation time, not by `benchmarks/graph/scripts/aggregate.py`. `benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md` §Recommendations #5 and §Methodology notes both flag this as a gap:\n\n> This sweep needed an ad-hoc transcript pass to surface the headline finding. `aggregate.py` should emit a per-arm tool-call-mix column so the next sweep reports utilization alongside tokens and pass-rate.\n\nWithout utilization as a first-class output, every future sweep will need the same ad-hoc pass, and the benchmark's primary table will continue to mislead a casual reader: cost parity between `hybrid` and `no-graph` only means something once you know whether the tools were actually invoked.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nThis task is a prerequisite to v2 reporting. The two in-flight v2 prep tasks (tool-surface trim T20260423-0506 and v2 fixture design T20260423-0507) are both trying to *move* utilization. Without a first-class utilization column, we cannot tell whether either intervention worked — we'd be back to an ad-hoc scan after a multi-hour sweep.\n\nIt also makes past versions re-readable: running `make -C benchmarks graph-aggregate GRAPH_VERSION=v1` should regenerate v1's tables *with* utilization, matching the numbers hand-computed in `benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md` §Tool-utilization audit.\n\n## Proposal\n\nExtend `benchmarks/graph/scripts/aggregate.py` to read the transcript sibling of each run record (`<seed>.transcript.json`) and classify tool uses, then surface the classification as columns on the primary table.\n\nClassifier logic (matches the ad-hoc v1 scan):\n\n- **Claude transcripts.** Each `message.content` block with `type == \"tool_use\"` counts. A call is a graph call if `name.startswith(\"mcp__orbit-bench__orbit_graph_\")`. Filesystem tools are names in `{Read, Grep, Glob}`. Shell is `name == \"Bash\"`.\n- **Codex transcripts.** Items with `type == \"item.completed\"` and a subtype of `mcp_tool_call` → match `orbit_graph_` prefix for graph calls. Items of subtype `command_execution` count as shell.\n- **Unknown tool names** go into an `other` bucket rather than being silently dropped; a non-zero `other` count in the aggregate report is a hint that the classifier needs updating.\n\nNew columns on the primary table (per `(provider, arm, task_class)` cell):\n\n- `graph_calls` — total graph-tool calls across runs in the cell.\n- `graph_call_rate` — fraction of runs in the cell that made at least one graph call (e.g. `1/30 = 3.3%`).\n- `shell_or_fs_calls` — total filesystem / shell calls across runs in the cell. Folds Claude's Grep/Read/Glob and Codex's `command_execution` into one counter since they play the same role.\n\nSecondary breakdown table can stay as-is unless trivial to extend.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Must preserve the current columns on the primary table (`runs`, `pass_rate`, `median_total_tokens`, `p90_total_tokens`, `tokens_per_success`) in the same order, with the new columns appended. `benchmarks/CONVENTIONS.md` §`RESULTS.md` required sections lists exactly those columns; adding trailing columns is fine, reshuffling is not.\n- Must be invocable against a frozen snapshot: `make -C benchmarks graph-aggregate GRAPH_VERSION=v1` should regenerate v1's table with utilization. Numbers must match the hand-computed values in `benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md` §Tool-utilization audit (Claude hybrid: 1 graph call across 30 runs; Codex hybrid: 0 across 30).\n- Do NOT add new deps to `benchmarks/graph/scripts/`. The existing Python + PyYAML stack is sufficient.\n- Missing transcript file (transcript not captured, or older run record) should not crash the aggregator — the cell prints `graph_calls = -` (or `N/A`) and continues. This keeps the tool usable mid-sweep.\n- Classifier changes are exactly the kind of thing that should be unit-tested (per `feedback_tests_for_complex_logic.md`). Write the tests.\n\n## Validation Plan\n\n1. Regenerate `benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md` §Tool-utilization audit values by running `make -C benchmarks graph-aggregate GRAPH_VERSION=v1`. The cell `(claude, hybrid, locate)` must show exactly 1 graph call across 10 runs; `(codex, hybrid, *)` must show 0 graph calls everywhere. If the numbers drift, the classifier is wrong.\n2. Run against a mock transcript fixture with one known call of each kind; assert exact counts.\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Reshaping the primary table format (column reorder, header rename).\n- Redoing the tool-utilization analysis for fixtures under `benchmarks/graph_v1/` — that data stays as-is; the regenerated table just matches it.\n- Adding a separate utilization table; the requirement is a column on the existing primary table.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Extended `benchmarks/graph/scripts/aggregate.py` to read each run's `<seed>.transcript.json` sibling, classify tool use into `graph_calls`, `shell_or_fs_calls`, and `other_calls`, and append `graph_calls`, `graph_call_rate`, and `shell_or_fs_calls` to the primary aggregate table without disturbing the existing columns.\n- Added `benchmarks/graph/scripts/test_aggregate.py` to cover Claude and Codex transcript classification, missing-transcript rendering, and stderr reporting for `other` tool-use events.\n- Updated `benchmarks/Makefile` so `graph-aggregate` and `graph-test` reuse the living harness scripts against version-selected data directories, which lets `GRAPH_VERSION=v1` pick up the new aggregate columns without modifying `benchmarks/graph_v1/`.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Kept the secondary `(provider, model, arm, task_class)` table unchanged. | Rationale: the task explicitly allowed this, and limiting the change to the primary table kept the reporting diff focused.\n- Routed frozen-version aggregation through the living script via Makefile rather than editing frozen snapshot scripts. | Rationale: this satisfies the task acceptance criteria and matches `benchmarks/CONVENTIONS.md` guidance about addressing frozen versions without copying code.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- The v1 aggregate now reports `43` `other` tool-use events to stderr, all from Claude `ToolSearch` usage outside the graph/filesystem/shell buckets. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: the warning is intentional and keeps the classifier honest; a future task can split `other` into finer categories if the report needs that breakdown.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe implementation meets the task goal with low churn: the frozen v1 aggregate now reproduces the expected hybrid graph-call counts, missing transcripts degrade gracefully, and the benchmark script coverage stays green.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nThe v1 graph benchmark fixture set is grep-skewed: 5 of 6 fixtures can be solved with `rg` in 1–2 calls. This is documented in `benchmarks/graph_v1/METHOD.md` §Known Caveats #3 and `RESULTS.md` §Recommendations #4. Consequence: v1's 1.7% hybrid graph-tool utilization rate is partly selection bias, not a graph-tool indictment. A v2 sweep run against the same fixture shape will reproduce the same null result even if the tool surface is improved.\n\nTo get a real read on graph-tool value (or lack of it), v2 needs fixtures where **grep is structurally the wrong tool** — cases where lexical search produces ambiguous or noisy results and a structural index is load-bearing. The one real accuracy signal in v1 (Codex graph-only: 80% → 100% on locate/trace) came from exactly two fixtures that had a grep-trap: `locate-v2-runtime-host-trait` (filename collision between the trait def and the impl) and `trace-policy-denial-wiring` (construct-vs-destructure disambiguation). v2 needs more fixtures of that shape.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nWithout this fixture work, v2 cannot distinguish \"graph tools are genuinely underused\" from \"we haven't measured them on the work they're actually good at.\" Either conclusion has large downstream consequences — including the possibility of deprecating the tools entirely — and the current fixture set cannot support either answer.\n\nThis task is the second half of the v2 setup; the first half is the tool-surface trim (separate task). A v2 sweep should only be run once both have landed.\n\n## Proposal\n\nAdd at least 4 new fixtures under `benchmarks/graph/tasks/` covering categories where grep is structurally wrong:\n\n1. **Cross-crate trait-impl walk under name collision.** Two traits with the same short name in different crates (e.g. two `Runtime`-ish traits); the task asks about implementors of one specifically. `rg \"impl Runtime\"` returns ambiguous hits; the graph's typed edges disambiguate.\n\n2. **Transitive caller query.** \"List every function that eventually calls `X` (walk the call graph N hops).\" Grep can only do one hop at a time and loses context across function boundaries. Uses `orbit.graph.callers` structurally.\n\n3. **Construct-site vs. pattern-match disambiguation across many variants.** Stronger version of `trace-policy-denial-wiring`: pick an enum with 6+ variants where each variant has both construct and pattern-match sites; ask for construct sites only on a specific subset of variants.\n\n4. **Refactor-impact across the type graph.** \"If the field `X` on type `T` is renamed, which files need changes?\" Includes struct-literal construction sites, dotted-access reads, and pattern-destructuring — grep on the field name alone returns false positives from unrelated types with the same field name.\n\nEach fixture must ship with:\n\n- A `why-grep-fails.md` sibling note (or a section in a single `V2_FIXTURES.md`) explaining the specific grep trap and citing the ground-truth set.\n- An oracle (`grep` with `must_include` + `must_not_include`, matching the v1 schema) strong enough to reject a naive grep-based answer.\n- A pinned `commit_sha` matching the v2 harness SHA.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Fixtures live at `benchmarks/graph/tasks/<id>.yaml` and must conform to `benchmarks/graph/tasks/_schema.yaml`. Do not modify the schema in this task without flagging it as a scope expansion.\n- Oracles must use the `grep` form (not `judge`) for v2 parity with v1. Judge-based oracles are out of scope.\n- Each `must_not_include` list should deliberately include the top 3 grep false positives for that fixture — that is what makes the fixture rigorous; see `trace-policy-denial-wiring.yaml` for the pattern.\n- Fixtures must exercise the post-trim tool surface (separate task). If that task changes `orbit.graph.overview` defaults or removes a tool, fixtures must not implicitly depend on the old shape.\n- Do NOT delete any v1 fixtures in this task. The v1 set stays available for longitudinal comparison under `benchmarks/graph_v1/tasks/`. New fixtures land in the living harness at `benchmarks/graph/tasks/`.\n- Do NOT run a v2 sweep in this task. Sweep execution is a separate task once both the surface trim and the fixtures are in place.\n\n## Validation Plan\n\nBefore calling the task done:\n\n1. Sanity-check each fixture by hand: run `grep -rn <naive-pattern>` against the repo at the pinned SHA and confirm the raw grep output would fail the oracle (i.e. contains a `must_not_include` hit or misses a `must_include` hit).\n2. Run each fixture under `graph-only` for 1 seed each provider and confirm at least one provider passes. If neither does, the fixture is either under-specified or the tool surface genuinely cannot solve it — flag and discuss.\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Modifying the fixture schema.\n- v2 sweep execution (that is the follow-up task after this one and the surface trim both land).\n- Updating `aggregate.py` to report tool-utilization inline (v1 RESULTS.md recommendation #5 — can be filed as a separate small task).",
    "execution_summary": "## v2 Graph Benchmark Fixtures — Execution Summary\n\n### Delivered (4 new fixtures, 3 distinct classes)\n\n| task_id | class | difficulty | Gate C pass |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `callers-run-deterministic-containers` | trace | medium | claude sonnet seed 1 |\n| `trace-tool-call-event-construct-sites` | trace | medium | codex seed 1 |\n| `impact-tool-context-struct-literals` | impact | medium | claude **opus** budget 4 seed 1 |\n| `locate-loopaudit-variants` | locate | easy | claude sonnet seed 1 |\n\nPlus `benchmarks/graph/V2_FIXTURES.md` with grep-failure rationale, Gate B evidence, and Gate C run records for each fixture.\n\n### Gate B (naive-grep counter-check) results\n\nEach fixture's naive `rg` pattern was executed against HEAD and its output compared to the oracle:\n\n- **callers**: grep returns 5 call lines but not containing fn names; oracle asks for fn names.\n- **trace**: grep returns 5 `LoopAuditEvent::ToolCall*` matches mixing construct (2 target lines in `agent_loop.rs`) and destructure (3 false-positive sites in `tool_enforcement.rs` + `v2_job_runtime_smoke.rs`). A naive answer fails 3 `must_not_include` entries.\n- **impact**: grep for `ToolContext` returns 240 hits across 95 files. The correct answer is 5 files. Reverted via `git checkout .gitignore`.     "plan": "# Plan — v2 graph benchmark fixtures\n\n## Ordering rationale\n\nFixture design is cheap to get wrong and expensive to re-benchmark, so this plan front-loads **outline → validate → implement** rather than jumping straight to YAML. The cheapest step (outline in markdown) exposes whether the fixture is well-defined; the next-cheapest step (naive-grep counter-check) catches false-positive fixtures before any sweep runs.\n\nEach candidate fixture passes through three gates before landing:\n\n1. **Gate A — outlined.** Has a clear prompt, ground truth, and hypothesized grep failure mode.\n2. **Gate B — grep-falsified.** Running the naive grep pattern at the pinned SHA demonstrably produces an answer that fails the oracle (either hits `must_not_include` or misses `must_include`).\n3. **Gate C — graph-passable.** At least one provider passes under `graph-only` at seed 1. If no provider passes, the fixture goes back to Gate A.\n\n## Step 1 — Establish working directory and SHA pin\n\n- Confirm the current living-harness SHA: `git rev-parse HEAD` at repo root. Pin this SHA in every new fixture's `commit_sha` field.\n- Create `benchmarks/graph/V2_FIXTURES.md` as an empty scaffold with sections for each candidate fixture (populated in Step 2).\n- Read `benchmarks/graph/tasks/_schema.yaml` and `benchmarks/graph/tasks/trace-policy-denial-wiring.yaml` end-to-end as templates; all new fixtures must match that shape.\n\n## Step 2 — Outline 6 candidate fixtures (target 4 landed)\n\nAuthor 6 candidates to account for attrition at Gates B and C. One per category, plus 2 spares.\n\nFor each candidate, write a section in `V2_FIXTURES.md` with:\n\n- **Task ID** (kebab-case).\n- **Class** (one of `locate | trace | impact | deps`).\n- **Naive grep pattern** — the exact `rg` invocation an agent would reach for first.\n- **Expected grep failure mode** — why the output is wrong or ambiguous. Specific and verifiable.\n- **Ground truth** — the exact set of paths/symbols the agent must list. Hand-curated from the repo at the pinned SHA.\n- **Graph-tool path** — which `orbit.graph.*` tool(s) resolve the ambiguity, and in what sequence.\n\n### Candidate pool (6 to draft, 4+ to land)\n\n1. **`locate-runtime-name-collision`** (locate) — Two traits with the short name `Runtime` exist in different crates. Ask for the trait used by a specific caller. Grep `trait Runtime` returns both; the graph distinguishes via typed edges.\n\n2. **`callers-transitive-policy-check`** (trace) — \"List every function that transitively calls `OrbitPolicy::check_read` within 3 hops, across crates.\" Grep can name direct callers only; `orbit.graph.callers` walks the edge graph structurally.\n\n3. **`trace-audit-event-construct-multi-variant`** (trace) — Stronger version of `trace-policy-denial-wiring`. Pick `LoopAuditEvent` (has ~8 variants); ask for construct-sites on a specific 3-variant subset. Grep on variant names returns construct + pattern sites intermixed.\n\n4. **`impact-rename-tool-context-field`** (impact) — \"If the field `X` on `ToolContext` is renamed, which files need changes?\" Must include struct-literal constructions, dotted reads, `TypeName { X, .. }` patterns. Grep on the bare field name returns false positives from unrelated types with the same field.\n\n5. **`deps-reverse-dependents-orbit-common`** (deps) — \"Which crates reverse-depend on `orbit-common`?\" Grep on `orbit-common` in Cargo.toml files works, but misses the distinction between `path =` and `version =` deps, and doesn't include dev/build dependency edges. The graph answers this directly.\n\n6. **`locate-impl-under-generic-constraint`** (locate) — A trait with impls that are `impl<T: Bound> Trait for Foo<T>`. Grep `impl Trait for Foo` misses the generic forms. Spare candidate — prioritize landing if one of 1–5 fails Gate B.\n\nPick 4 to carry forward after Step 3.\n\n## Step 3 — Gate B: naive-grep counter-check\n\nFor each candidate from Step 2, at the pinned SHA:\n\n- Run the documented naive `rg` pattern against the repo.\n- Record the top 10 hits verbatim in the candidate's `V2_FIXTURES.md` section.\n- Compare against the hand-curated ground truth. Confirm **at least one** of:\n  - A `must_not_include` path appears in the grep output (false positive), OR\n  - A `must_include` path is absent from the grep output (false negative).\n\nIf neither, the fixture is not grep-hard — drop it. Move to a spare.\n\n**Output of this step:** a reduced list of 4–5 fixtures that are provably grep-hard.\n\n## Step 4 — Write YAML fixtures\n\nFor each surviving fixture, author `benchmarks/graph/tasks/<task_id>.yaml` matching `_schema.yaml`:\n\n- `task_id`: kebab-case, matches filename.\n- `class`: from `{locate, trace, impact, deps}`. Ensure the final set covers ≥2 distinct classes (AC#8).\n- `difficulty`: `medium` or `hard` (v2 should push harder than v1's easy-leaning set).\n- `commit_sha`: the SHA pinned in Step 1.\n- `prompt`: verbatim user message. Be explicit about output format (path lists, counts, etc.) — follow `trace-policy-denial-wiring.yaml` as the style template.\n- `oracle.grep`:\n  - `must_include`: the hand-curated ground-truth paths/tokens.\n  - `must_not_include`: the top 3+ grep false positives identified in Step 3 (this is what makes the oracle rigorous — AC#3).\n\n## Step 5 — Gate C: graph-only smoke test\n\nFor each fixture, run:\n\n```\nbenchmarks/graph/scripts/run.sh graph-only <task_id> 1 --provider claude\nbenchmarks/graph/scripts/run.sh graph-only <task_id> 1 --provider codex\n```\n\nCheck `benchmarks/graph/runs/<provider>/graph-only/<task_id>/1.json` for `verdict: pass`.\n\n- If at least one provider passes: fixture lands.\n- If neither provider passes: add a `status: needs-refinement` note to the fixture's `V2_FIXTURES.md` entry and move to the next spare. Do not land a fixture that no provider can solve — that's a harness problem, not a measurement.\n\nThis step is a one-shot check at seed 1, not a full 5-seed sweep. The full sweep is the separate follow-up task.\n\n## Step 6 — Update `V2_FIXTURES.md` with final shape\n\nFor each landed fixture, record in `V2_FIXTURES.md`:\n\n- (a) the naive grep pattern\n- (b) why it fails (concrete, not abstract)\n- (c) which graph tool(s) resolve it\n- (d) the Gate B counter-check result (grep output vs. ground truth)\n- (e) the Gate C smoke test result (claude pass / codex pass / both / one)\n\nThis satisfies AC#4 and AC#5 in one document.\n\n## Step 7 — Update `benchmarks/graph/README.md`\n\nAdd a `§Tasks` reference to `V2_FIXTURES.md`:\n\n> The v2 fixture set (additions since `graph_v1/`) is documented in [`V2_FIXTURES.md`](./V2_FIXTURES.md), including the grep-failure rationale for each new fixture.\n\nThis satisfies AC#10.\n\n## Step 8 — Final checks\n\n- Confirm `git diff benchmarks/graph_v1/` is empty (AC#7).\n- Confirm `git diff benchmarks/CONVENTIONS.md` is empty (AC#9).\n- Count landed fixtures: ≥4 (AC#1).\n- Count distinct classes across landed fixtures: ≥2 (AC#8).\n- Every landed YAML validates against `_schema.yaml` (AC#2) — spot-check by opening each file.\n- Every landed fixture's `must_not_include` has ≥3 entries corresponding to real grep false positives from Step 3 (AC#3).\n\n## Risks and mitigations\n\n- **Ground-truth drift mid-task.** The living workspace may move while this task is open, changing grep output or oracle truth. Mitigation: pin the SHA in Step 1 and re-check it before Step 5; if the repo has moved, re-run Gate B.\n- **A candidate looks grep-hard but Codex/Claude just grep-solve-it-anyway via cleverness.** This is the most likely failure mode. Mitigation: the `must_not_include` list must be tight enough that a grep agent's most natural path hits one of them. If Codex passes a \"grep-hard\" fixture with a single `rg` call, the fixture is not actually grep-hard — drop it.\n- **Overfitting to current implementations.** If a fixture is authored against the v1 repo shape and a refactor changes the ground truth, the fixture rots. Mitigation: the pinned `commit_sha` makes this explicit and auditable; future re-validations can re-pin.\n- **Fixture count inflation.** Don't add more than ~6 new fixtures to v2. Each fixture adds ~5–10 minutes per seed per arm per provider, and sweep cost multiplies fast. If the pool shrinks below 4 after Gates B + C, file a follow-up rather than landing weak fixtures.\n\n## Explicitly deferred\n\n- Running the full v2 sweep (separate follow-up).\n- Adding tool-utilization columns to `aggregate.py` (separate follow-up — v1 RESULTS.md recommendation #5).\n- Modifying any file under `benchmarks/graph_v1/`.\n- Modifying `benchmarks/graph/tasks/_schema.yaml`.\n- Authoring judge-based oracles; all v2 oracles use the `grep` form for parity with v1.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nThe v1 graph benchmark (`benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md`) found that agents invoked graph tools in only 1 of 60 hybrid runs (1.7% utilization), and `benchmarks/graph_v1/ISSUES.md` documented specific tool-surface defects that make graph tools the higher-friction choice vs. grep:\n\n- `orbit.graph.overview` returned 65,756 chars (~16K tokens) for a narrow symbol-location task — dumping 47 files + 427 symbols when a targeted query was already in hand.\n- `orbit.graph.search \"AgentRuntime\"` returned `benchmarks/graph/tasks/locate-agentruntime.yaml` config keys ahead of code symbols. Unfiltered search ranks non-code hits too high.\n- `orbit.graph.refs` on the `AgentRuntime` trait (6,183 chars / ~1,546 tokens) mixed README hits, doc sections, and code refs in one undifferentiated list.\n- `orbit.graph.pack` returned full impl bodies that the model then restated almost verbatim downstream — a signature-only summary would have been sufficient.\n- Tool descriptions are present-tense feature lists (\"Return an aggregate overview...\"). They do not tell the agent *when to prefer this over grep*. The one successful v1 hybrid graph call (`orbit_graph_implementors` on `AgentRuntime`) worked because that tool's purpose is unambiguous from its name.\n\nSeparately, under forced `graph-only`, the MCP schema accumulated 1.5M–3.1M `cache_read_tokens` per task class. This is invisible in hybrid (because the tools aren't used) but becomes real cost the moment utilization rises. The only lever within our control is reducing the schema footprint.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nThe v1 utilization rate makes a v2 sweep uninformative until the surface is fixed — v2 will reproduce v1's null result even with better fixtures if agents continue to route around graph tools. This task is a prerequisite to task follow-up on v2 fixture design (filed separately).\n\nThere is also a real accuracy signal in v1 (Codex graph-only: 80% → 100% pass-rate on locate/trace) that is currently only accessible by *forcing* graph-only. Making the tools choose-worthy in hybrid captures that accuracy lift without forcing mode, which is the production shape we want.\n\n## Proposal\n\nFour coordinated changes to `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_*.rs`:\n\n1. **`orbit.graph.search`** — default-strict relevance. When `type` / `kind` / `prefix` are all unset, rank code-symbol hits above file-path and non-code hits, and cap returned non-code nodes to a small constant (e.g. 2) unless the caller opts in via a new `include_non_code: bool = false` flag.\n\n2. **`orbit.graph.overview`** — either (a) deprecate if `search + show + implementors` covers the use case, or (b) change the default output to the already-existing `format: summary` mode and require `format: full` to get the current verbose shape. ISSUES.md recommendation §Recommendations #1 explicitly says \"do not call `orbit.graph.overview` after `orbit.graph.search` already found the target symbol\" — the tool encourages the wrong path by default.\n\n3. **`orbit.graph.refs`** — partition the output into `{ code_refs: [...], doc_refs: [...], config_refs: [...] }`. Default the response to `code_refs` only; callers opt in to `include: [\"doc\", \"config\"]` for the rest. Cap per-file hit count.\n\n4. **`orbit.graph.pack`** — add `summary: bool = true` default returning signatures + file paths only; callers pass `summary: false` to get full bodies.\n\nPlus across all `orbit.graph.*` tool schemas: rewrite each `description` field to lead with \"Use when …\" and include a \"Prefer over grep when …\" clause naming what grep cannot answer (filename collisions, construct-vs-destructure, cross-crate impl walks, transitive callers). Remove decorative prose; every token is paid on every turn.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Existing callers in `crates/orbit-core/assets/skills/orbit-graph/SKILL.md` and `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/mcp/mod.rs` must continue to work. Where defaults change, update the skill doc and any test fixtures in the same PR.\n- Do NOT change `orbit-knowledge` service-layer signatures unless strictly necessary — the filtering / partitioning should happen in the `orbit-tools` builtin layer so the service stays a thin data layer (per the CLAUDE.md crate architecture).\n- No new cross-crate deps. This work is confined to `orbit-tools` (and possibly `orbit-knowledge` if a partitioning helper genuinely belongs there).\n- Measure the schema-token reduction before/after; it's a primary observable (see AC#7).\n- Do not land this together with v2 fixture work (filed separately). These should be two independent commits so a v2 sweep can A/B the surface trim independently of the fixture change if needed.\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Actual v2 fixture authoring (separate task).\n- Indexer-side exclusion via `.orbitignore` (already filed as T20260423-0452; a complementary fix but independently shippable).\n- Adding new graph tools. This task is scope reduction + shape fixes only.",

    "plan": "# Plan — Trim orbit.graph.* tool surface\n\n> **Related:** Tool-description rewrites were split into T20260423-0525 (pure-text change, ships independently). That task supersedes the original Step 2 of this plan. The schema-size baseline (Step 1 below) should be taken AFTER T-0525 lands so description savings are attributed to T-0525, not to this task.\n\n## Ordering rationale\n\nThe changes are sequenced lowest-risk → highest-risk so that regression signal from early steps informs later steps, and so the schema-size measurement (AC#7) has a clean baseline before any shape changes land.\n\nEach step below ends in a green `cargo test -p orbit-tools` so partial progress is always landable.\n\n## Step 1 — Baseline measurement + schema-size harness\n\n**Goal:** make AC#7 (≥25% schema-size reduction) measurable from day one.\n\n**Precondition:** T20260423-0525 (description rewrite) has landed. Baseline is measured against the post-T-0525 state so this task's reduction budget targets shape changes, not wording.\n\n- Add `crates/orbit-tools/tests/graph_schema_size.rs` (integration test). It iterates every `Tool` whose `schema().name.starts_with(\"orbit.graph.\")`, serializes each `ToolSchema` via `serde_json::to_string`, and sums the byte count.\n- The test prints the total to stdout and asserts the sum is below a `MAX_BYTES` constant. On first commit, set `MAX_BYTES` to the current total so the test passes; subsequent commits lower it as shape changes land.\n- Capture the baseline number in the PR description; final PR shows before/after with ≥25% delta.\n\n**Files:** new `crates/orbit-tools/tests/graph_schema_size.rs`, one const.\n\n**Verify:** `cargo test -p orbit-tools graph_schema_size` prints the baseline and passes.\n\n## Step 2 — `orbit.graph.pack` signature-only default (AC#4)\n\n**Goal:** simplest shape change — an input default flip with a new branch in the execution path.\n\n- Add `summary: bool` param (default `true`) to the schema in `knowledge_pack.rs`.\n- In `execute`, if `summary` is true, walk the packed response and strip body text from each entry, keeping `{name, kind, file, line, signature}` only. A helper in `orbit_knowledge::store::pack` (if one fits naturally) is acceptable; otherwise inline in the tool.\n- Unit test: fixture graph with one impl block; assert response JSON for the default call does NOT contain the impl body string, and that `summary: false` DOES.\n\n**Files:** `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_pack.rs`, possibly a small helper in `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/store/pack.rs`.\n\n**Verify:** new unit test passes; schema-size test still green.\n\n## Step 3 — `orbit.graph.refs` output partitioning (AC#3)\n\n**Goal:** shape the response into `{code_refs, doc_refs, config_refs}`. This is the highest-value single change from the ISSUES.md evidence (the `AgentRuntime` refs call returned doc + code mixed).\n\n- In `knowledge_refs.rs`, classify each ref by its node's location: `*.rs|*.py|*.ts|…` → `code_refs`; `*.md|*.txt|docs/` → `doc_refs`; `*.yaml|*.toml|*.json|config/` → `config_refs`. Centralize the classifier as a small helper (`fn classify_ref_kind(path: &Path) -> RefKind`) co-located with the tool.\n- Add an `include: Vec<String>` optional param defaulting to `[\"code\"]`. If the caller passes `[\"code\",\"doc\",\"config\"]` or `[\"all\"]`, include all three.\n- Response shape: always the top-level keys `{code_refs, doc_refs, config_refs}`, but `doc_refs` and `config_refs` are `[]` unless requested.\n- Add per-file cap: no single file contributes more than `per_file_limit` (default 5) refs in any category.\n- Unit test: fixture graph where a selector has 3 code refs, 2 doc refs, 1 config ref. Assert default call returns `code_refs: [3 items], doc_refs: [], config_refs: []`. Assert `include: [\"all\"]` returns all three populated.\n\n**Files:** `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_refs.rs`, test fixtures.\n\n**Verify:** new unit test passes; schema-size test still green.\n\n## Step 4 — `orbit.graph.search` ranking (AC#1, AC#10)\n\n**Goal:** code symbols rank above non-code hits when filters are unset.\n\n- In `knowledge_search.rs`, when `type`/`kind`/`prefix` are all absent, wrap results with a rank key: code symbols (kind in `{function, method, struct, trait, impl, class, interface, field, module}`) get rank 0; file-path matches get rank 1; non-code nodes (YAML/MD/TOML by extension) get rank 2.\n- Sort stable-by-rank before applying `limit`.\n- Add `include_non_code: bool` default `false`. When false AND no explicit filters, drop non-code results entirely from the response (don't just deprioritize — remove, so the `limit` window isn't polluted).\n- Unit test: fixture graph with one `trait AgentRuntime` symbol and one `benchmarks/graph/tasks/locate-agentruntime.yaml` file node. Query `AgentRuntime`, default params; assert the symbol is the top-1 result and the YAML node is absent from the response.\n- Smoke test for AC#10: after Step 4 lands, run `orbit tool run orbit.graph.search --input '{\"query\":\"AgentRuntime\",\"limit\":5}'` against the live orbit workspace and capture the output in the PR description.\n\n**Files:** `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_search.rs`, unit test, a `rank_by_kind` helper.\n\n**Verify:** new unit test passes; smoke test output recorded.\n\n## Step 5 — `orbit.graph.overview` decision gate (AC#2, AC#6)\n\n**Goal:** resolve the keep-or-remove question explicitly.\n\n- Before implementing, write a short note at the top of the PR description: \"Decision: keep / deprecate. Reason: …\"\n- Recommended default based on ISSUES.md evidence: **keep but flip the default to `format: \"summary\"` when the query scope is unbounded OR contains > 20 files.** The current tool already supports a summary mode; the change is the default behavior.\n- Implement: in `knowledge_overview.rs::OverviewFormat::parse`, when `format` is not provided, compute file count for the requested scope; if > 20 or scope is unbounded, return `Summary`; otherwise `Full`. Keep the existing `FILE_THRESHOLD = 50` auto-compact behavior as an additional backstop.\n- Unit test: fixture graph with 25 files, call with no prefix and no format; assert response does not contain per-file symbol arrays (i.e. is the summary shape).\n- If the implementing agent decides to deprecate instead: remove from the registry in `builtin/orbit/mod.rs`, delete `knowledge_overview.rs`, add a migration hint to the `orbit-graph` skill doc, and note the removal in the PR description. Skip the unit test above; instead add a test asserting the tool is NOT registered.\n\n**Files:** `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/knowledge_overview.rs`, tests.\n\n**Verify:** new unit test passes; schema-size test ideally shows a meaningful drop here.\n\n## Step 6 — Skill doc sync (AC#9)\n\n- Open `crates/orbit-core/assets/skills/orbit-graph/SKILL.md` and verify every command example still matches the new tool shapes.     "description": "## Problem\n\nThe knowledge-graph indexer at `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/pipeline/scan.rs` filters files using `git check-ignore --stdin` only (see `git_ignored_paths` at line 78). Files that are committed to git but should not be indexed have no exclusion path.\n\nThe motivating case is the frozen benchmark snapshot committed as part of T20260422-1609: `benchmarks/graph_v1/runs/` contains 373 per-run JSON records and JSONL transcripts (~6 MB) that are tracked in git for reproducibility (see `benchmarks/CONVENTIONS.md` §\"Runs and git\"). Without a second-layer filter, these files reach the parser and pollute the graph.\n\nThis pollution was directly observed in v1: `orbit.graph.search \"AgentRuntime\"` returned `benchmarks/graph/tasks/locate-agentruntime.yaml` config keys *before* code symbols — evidence captured in `benchmarks/graph_v1/ISSUES.md`.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- **Graph quality.** Transcript strings produce thousands of spurious symbols/refs that dilute search ranking and bloat the store.\n- **Parse time.** Every full reindex reparses the frozen artifacts; they are append-only and never change.\n- **Gates v2 benchmark design.** The next round of graph benchmark fixtures (see `benchmarks/graph_v1/RESULTS.md` §Recommendations #4) will add navigation-heavy tasks whose ground truth assumes search does not return benchmark noise. Without this filter, v2 measurements would partly be measuring indexer noise rather than graph-tool utility.\n- **User extensibility.** Workspaces with committed-but-non-code artifacts (test fixtures, golden files, captured API responses) need a supported way to opt out of indexing without having to gitignore them.\n\n## Proposal\n\nIntroduce `.orbitignore` — a gitignore-compatible exclusion file that the knowledge-graph indexer consults before queueing files for parse. Semantics match gitignore (`**` globs, negation `!pattern`, directory-only `foo/`, `#` comments). Nested `.orbitignore` files in subdirectories compose with the root file.\n\nUse the `ignore` crate (same as ripgrep) to get battle-tested, gitignore-compatible pattern logic without hand-rolling. This dependency belongs in `orbit-knowledge` per the architecture diagram in `CLAUDE.md`.\n\n## Layer Separation\n\n`.orbitignore` is NOT the same as policy `denyRead` / `denyModify`:\n\n| Layer | Purpose | When evaluated |\n|---|---|---|\n| `.orbitignore` | Indexer inclusion — controls what enters the knowledge graph | At parse/scan time |\n| Policy `denyRead` / `denyModify` | Runtime FS access at activity execution | At tool-call time |\n\nBoth can reference the same path but answer different questions. Docs must make this separation explicit so users don't conflate them.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Lives in `orbit-knowledge` (the pipeline crate). Per `CLAUDE.md`, that crate depends on `orbit-common` only and has no downstream code-path back into the deny-layer, so the separation is structural.\n- Default ignore set should ship out-of-box: at minimum `target/`, `node_modules/`, `.orbit/`. Users should not have to boilerplate these.\n- Incremental reindex after `.orbitignore` changes: adding a pattern that excludes a previously-indexed path must remove that path's nodes on the next pipeline run without requiring a full rebuild. Removing a pattern must re-admit the path.\n- This task is feature work, not friction. It is authored from a user-visible gap (benchmark-run pollution is observable in `graph.search` output), not an agent self-report.\n- Ownership: `claude` owns `docs/design/knowledge-graph/` per `CLAUDE.md`. The layer-separation doc section goes there.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\nImplemented scan-time `.orbitignore` support in `orbit-knowledge` using the `ignore` crate, with built-in defaults plus root and nested `.orbitignore` composition. Added unit coverage in `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/pipeline/scan.rs` and integration coverage in `crates/orbit-knowledge/tests/orbitignore.rs` for literal matches, recursive globs, negation, directory-only rules, comments, default ignores, nested composition, and incremental reindex removal.\n\nAdded workspace-root `.orbitignore` seeding during `orbit workspace init` in `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/workspace.rs`, backed by a shared default template exported from `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/lib.rs`, while preserving existing user-authored `.orbitignore` files.\n\nUpdated `docs/design/knowledge-graph/2_design.md`, `4_decisions.md`, and `references/glossary.md` to document the new scan-time inclusion layer and its separation from runtime policy access.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Keep `.orbitignore` scan-time behavior in `orbit-knowledge` and keep runtime access control in `orbit-policy`. | Rationale: preserves the architecture boundary and avoids coupling graph refresh to tool-call policy decisions.\n- Seed a visible workspace-root `.orbitignore` during `orbit workspace init` while preserving any existing file. | Rationale: users can discover and customize the defaults without reading source, but existing local intent is never overwritten.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe change is complete and verified. Search pollution from benchmark artifacts is removed by the new scan-time exclusion layer, default exclusions are visible/editable from workspace initialization onward, and the docs now describe the boundary between graph inclusion and runtime access control.\n\n## Verification\n- `make fmt`\n- `cargo test -p orbit-cli workspace_init`\n- `cargo test -p orbit-knowledge orbitignore`\n- `cargo test -p orbit-knowledge`\n- `make build`\n- `cargo build -p orbit-cli`\n- `target/debug/orbit workspace init --no-mcp` in a temp workspace to confirm seeded `.orbitignore` contents\n- `./target/debug/orbit graph build --root /Users/daniel/workspace/repos/orbit/.orbit --repo /Users/daniel/.codex/worktrees/430c/orbit --ref worktree-430c`\n- `./target/debug/orbit tool run orbit.graph.search --root /Users/daniel/workspace/repos/orbit/.orbit --full --input '{\"query\":\"AgentRuntime\",\"type\":\"symbol\",\"limit\":10,\"ref\":\"worktree-430c\",\"agent\":\"codex\",\"model\":\"gpt-5\"}'`\n\nThe final `orbit.graph.search` output returned only code symbols under `crates/orbit-agent/...` and no `benchmarks/**` hits.\n\n## Artifacts\n- Commit: `56117f6c` (`[T20260423-0452] Add .orbitignore scan exclusions and init seeding`)\n- Branch: `codex/t20260423-0452-orbitignore`\n",

    "description": "## Problem\n`orbit run duel` still exposes the retired execution entrypoint as the default path, and the preserved historical inspection surfaces are partially broken because `list` and latest `show` still require live duel job assets that no longer ship in the workspace.\n\n## Why It Matters\nUsers trying to inspect historical duel results are currently blocked by misleading default behavior and `job not found` failures, despite the CLI retirement message promising preserved scoreboard/list/show surfaces.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- Implement the fix in an isolated git worktree, not the primary dirty workspace.\n- Keep the command focused on historical inspection; do not resurrect duel execution workflows unless required.\n- Add regression coverage for the supported read-only surfaces under retired-job conditions.\n- Update any owned activity-job docs if the change embodies a non-obvious product decision.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\nRepaired the retired `orbit run duel` CLI so the bare command now defaults to the supported scoreboard surface instead of routing directly into the retired execution error path.\nChanged duel history loading for `list` and latest `show` to read stored job-run bundles through `list_job_runs(job_id=None)` and then filter duel job IDs, which preserves historical inspection even when live duel job assets are absent.\nUpdated audit metadata and CLI/help wording to reflect the new read-only default, refreshed README wording, and documented the stored-history decision in `docs/design/activity-job/2_design.md` and `4_decisions.md` with [T20260423-0447].\nAdded duel command regression tests covering:\n- bare `orbit run duel` aliases to scoreboard\n- explicit retired execution requests still error clearly\n- `list` and latest `show` work without live duel job assets\n- non-duel run IDs are still rejected by `show`\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Preserve old direct duel syntax only as a parser compatibility path that emits the explicit retirement error. | Rationale: this keeps existing user muscle memory from failing ambiguously while making the default path useful again.\n- Treat retired duel history as stored-data inspection rather than live asset resolution. | Rationale: historical observability should survive asset retirement.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe reported issues are resolved in the worktree branch. Bare `orbit run duel` is now usable as a read-only entrypoint, and the previous `job not found: job_duel_pipeline` failure on `list`/latest `show` is gone.\n\n## Validation\n- `make fmt`\n- `make build`\n- `cargo test -p orbit-cli duel`\n- `cargo run -p orbit-cli --bin orbit -- run duel --root /Users/daniel/workspace/repos/orbit/.orbit` -> `No duel runs recorded yet.`\n- `cargo run -p orbit-cli --bin orbit -- run duel list --root /Users/daniel/workspace/repos/orbit/.orbit` -> `No duel runs recorded yet.`\n- `cargo run -p orbit-cli --bin orbit -- run duel show --root /Users/daniel/workspace/repos/orbit/.orbit` -> `error: invalid input: no duel runs found`\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- Workspace build still emits pre-existing dead-code warnings in `orbit-store` and `orbit-engine`; they are unrelated to this fix but still visible during validation.\n",

    "description": "## Problem\nThe user-facing `orbit run ship` path dispatches `task_auto_pipeline` with only explicit CLI fields (`mode`, `base_branch`, `task_ids`, and optional `concurrency`). The v2 job executor in `crates/orbit-engine/src/activity_job/job_executor.rs` only applies `job.default_input` when the incoming input is `null`; when the caller supplies any object at all, the defaults are dropped instead of merged.\n\nIn practice, this breaks the seeded ship workflow immediately. `task_auto_pipeline.yaml` expects `input.max_tasks` in `list_backlog` and `input.max_bundle_size` in both `dispatch_agent` and `validate_bundles`. Running `orbit run ship T20260423-0436 --base agent-main --json` from QA task `T20260423-0437` failed at the first step with no worktree or PR side effects. Direct `orbit job run task_auto_pipeline` diagnostics surfaced the hidden template errors:\n- `missing input value for 'max_tasks'`\n- after supplying `max_tasks=50`, `missing input value for 'max_bundle_size'`\n\n## Why It Matters\n`orbit run ship` is supposed to be the default PR-shipping entrypoint in the README and CLI help. Right now it fails before task selection can complete, so the advertised workflow is not usable. The wrapper also hides the returned job `message`, making the failure hard to diagnose from `run ship show` alone.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- QA evidence: tracking task `T20260423-0437`, mock task `T20260423-0436`, failed ship runs `jrun-20260423-0438`, `jrun-20260423-0441`, and `jrun-20260423-0441-2`.\n- Fix the default-input behavior at the job-executor layer rather than hard-coding more keys into `orbit run ship`; other workflows rely on the same contract.\n- Preserve current explicit-input override behavior: caller-supplied keys should win, but unspecified keys should still inherit from `job.default_input`.\n- Consider surfacing `JobOutcome.message` in the persisted pipeline/`run ship show` path so future failures are diagnosable without reproducing via `orbit job run`.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\nUpdated the v2 job executor so object-valued caller input now shallow-merges over object-valued `job.default_input`, which preserves workflow defaults like `max_tasks` and `max_bundle_size` while still letting explicit caller keys win.\n\nPropagated failure detail through the execution path by carrying step-level messages into `JobOutcome.message` and persisting a synthetic failed job step for early v2 pipeline failures. This makes `orbit run ship --json` and `orbit run ship show --json` surface actionable error text instead of an empty failed summary.\n\nUpdated the activity-job design docs and added ADR-011 to capture the merged-input contract and the synthetic persisted failure-step decision under `[T20260423-0445]`.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Persisted early pipeline failures as a synthetic failed job step instead of widening the persisted pipeline schema. | Rationale: it reuses existing job-run summary plumbing and fixes the operator-facing CLI/debug surface with the smallest durable change.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- Successful end-to-end `task_auto_pipeline` execution was not validated in this environment because `dispatch_agent` currently fails later on missing provider credentials. | Severity: Medium | Mitigation: the original regression was reproduced and cleared, and the remaining failure now exposes the concrete credential error for follow-up debugging.\n\n## Deviations from Original Plan\n- Added deterministic executor unit tests in `job_executor.rs` in addition to the requested smoke validation. | Justification: they lock in the merge semantics at the bug seam so the workflow does not regress silently.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe reported regression is fixed at the shared executor layer, the user-facing ship workflow now preserves actionable failure detail, and the committed branch passed repo validation from the dedicated worktree.",

Gemini CLI supports project-local MCP configuration in `.gemini/settings.json`, so Orbit should be able to seed and remove the Orbit MCP server entry for the current workspace just like it now does for Claude and Codex.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Gemini should participate in the same MCP setup flow as Claude and Codex instead of being a manual follow-up.\n- Project-local Gemini config keeps the integration workspace-scoped rather than mutating global user settings in `~/.gemini/settings.json`.\n- This keeps the happy path coherent: `orbit workspace init` delegates to `orbit mcp init --auto`, and the Gemini portion of that path is implemented here when a repo opts into Gemini.\n\n## Scope\n\n1. **Gemini seeder under `mcp init`** — implement the Gemini-specific branch of `orbit mcp init --gemini` so it creates or updates project-local `.gemini/settings.json` with an Orbit MCP server entry for the current workspace.\n2. **Safe-default server registration** — the generated Gemini config should point at the current supported Orbit MCP command (`orbit serve mcp`) and rely on the server's default safe surface (graph read + stable `orbit.task.*` tools). This task should not add a separate client-side write opt-in path for experimental graph writes.\n3. **Remove path** — implement the Gemini-specific branch of `orbit mcp remove --gemini` so Orbit's Gemini MCP registration can be removed cleanly without disturbing unrelated project config.\n4. **Workspace init delegation** — ensure the Gemini seeder is what runs when `orbit workspace init` delegates to `orbit mcp init --auto` and Gemini is selected.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Do **not** modify the user's global `~/.gemini/settings.json`; this should stay workspace-local.\n- Keep unrelated `.gemini/settings.json` content intact when merging or removing the Orbit-owned server entry.\n- Because `.gemini/` will be a local workspace artifact, keep repo ignore/docs behavior consistent with the existing `.claude/` and `.codex/` integrations.\n- This task depends on the shared MCP scaffold/gating task and should not re-invent command parsing or provider selection logic locally.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nOrbit currently exposes only the low-level server entrypoint `orbit serve mcp`. Provider-specific integration work for Claude, Codex, and Gemini is being tracked separately, but there is no first-class Orbit command for installing or removing those client integrations, and the current `serve mcp` surface needs to remain safe-by-default for experimental graph writes.\n\nThat leaves us with two problems:\n\n- setup UX is fragmented across provider-specific ad hoc config work instead of a single Orbit command surface;\n- client-side bundles are not a real safety boundary, because any MCP client can point directly at `orbit serve mcp` unless the server itself defaults to a restricted tool surface.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Discoverability: `orbit mcp init` / `orbit mcp remove` gives users one obvious way to wire Orbit into Claude, Codex, or Gemini.\n- Happy path: `orbit workspace init` can still be the one-command entrypoint by delegating to `orbit mcp init --auto`.\n- Safety: the real gate needs to live in `orbit serve mcp`, with graph writes opt-in and stable task tools always available.\n- Sequencing: provider-specific seeders for Claude, Codex, and Gemini should be follow-ons under a shared scaffold rather than independent command surfaces.\n\n## Scope\n\n1. **Top-level MCP command group** — add a first-class `orbit mcp` command family with canonical verbs `init` and `remove`. Do not add `down` or aliases. `status` can remain out of scope unless explicitly approved later.\n2. **Provider selection** — `orbit mcp init` and `orbit mcp remove` auto-detect targets by default, with `--claude`, `--codex`, `--gemini`, and `--all` as explicit overrides. Auto mode should follow the proposed heuristics: `.claude/` and `.gemini/` indicate workspace integrations and `~/.codex/config.toml` indicates Codex availability.\n3. **Workspace init integration** — `orbit workspace init` should call the same `mcp init --auto` path by default, with `--no-mcp` as the escape hatch for users who do not want client integration seeded.\n4. **Server-side safety** — `orbit serve mcp` must default to a safe tool surface. Stable `orbit.task.*` tools remain exposed. Graph read tools remain exposed. Experimental graph write tools (`orbit.graph.add`, `orbit.graph.delete`, `orbit.graph.move`, `orbit.graph.write`) are hidden by default and appear only when the server is started with `--allow-write`.\n5. **Provider seeders as follow-ups** — Claude, Codex, and Gemini provider-specific file writes are implemented by dependent tasks rather than embedded as one-off logic in `workspace init`.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Do **not** add `--task` / `--graph` filtering flags to `orbit mcp init`; that is unnecessary surface area when the runtime policy and server-side gate already determine the actual tool exposure.\n- Client-side config remains useful for ergonomics and discoverability, but it is not the security boundary.\n- This task is the dependency root for the provider-specific seeding tasks currently tracked by T20260422-1621 (Claude), T20260422-1651 (Codex), and T20260422-1742 (Gemini).",
    "execution_summary": "Expanded the MCP scaffold to include Gemini alongside Claude and Codex: provider selection now supports `--gemini` and `--all`, auto-detect recognizes repo-local `.gemini/`, workspace init delegates through the same path, and the docs/help/tests were updated to reflect the three-provider flow while keeping the safe default MCP gate unchanged.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\n`orbit serve mcp` advertises all 12 `orbit.graph.*` tools over JSON-RPC stdio (verified by direct handshake — `tools/list` returns 69 tools including all graph ones). But when a child `claude -p` session is spawned with `--mcp-config` pointing at a config that declares the orbit server, the child sessions `init` event reports `mcp_servers: [{\"name\":\"orbit\",\"status\":\"disabled\"}]` and no `mcp__orbit__*` tools appear in the `tools` list, regardless of `--strict-mcp-config`.\n\n**Reproduction:**\n\n```json\n// benchmarks/graph/mcp.json\n{ \"mcpServers\": { \"orbit\": {\n  \"command\": \"/Users/daniel/.cargo/bin/orbit\",\n  \"args\": [\"serve\", \"mcp\"],\n  \"env\": { \"ORBIT_DATA_ROOT\": \"/Users/daniel/.orbit\" }\n}}}\n```\n\n```bash\nclaude -p \"call mcp__orbit__orbit_graph_overview\" \\\n  --output-format stream-json --verbose --no-session-persistence \\\n  --model sonnet --max-budget-usd 0.3 \\\n  --mcp-config benchmarks/graph/mcp.json --strict-mcp-config \\\n  --allowed-tools \"mcp__orbit__orbit_graph_overview\"\n```\n\nObserved init event: `{\"mcp_servers\":[{\"name\":\"orbit\",\"status\":\"disabled\"}]}`. Agent responds *\"The tool is not available in this environment — its not in the deferred tool list and wasnt found by search.\"*\n\nDirect JSON-RPC handshake against the same binary works fine:\n\n```bash\n(echo {\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"initialize\",...}; ...) | orbit serve mcp\n# → Total tools: 69; graph tools: [orbit.graph.add, ..., orbit.graph.write]\n```\n\nSo the server is healthy — the gap is in whatever Claude Code does to connect stdio MCP servers launched via `--mcp-config`. Possibly:\n\n1. `orbit serve mcp` needs additional stdio handshake Claude Code expects (e.g. initial Content-Length framing vs. line-delimited — though Claude Codes own docs indicate it speaks the standard MCP stdio transport).\n2. The server exits too quickly on connection error and Claude Code reports `status: disabled` instead of the actual error.\n3. An ambient project-level `disabledMcpServers: [\"orbit\"]` in `~/.claude.json` (confirmed present for this workspace) shadows the `--mcp-config` name collision. `--strict-mcp-config` docs say it should ignore ambient config but the `disabledMcpServers` block may still apply.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nThis blocks the knowledge-graph token-usage benchmark (task T20260422-1609). The `graph-only` and `hybrid` arms cannot actually expose graph tools to the agent under test when the benchmark runs through `claude -p`. The only thing that *appears* to work today is the `Agent` subagent tool, but that silently bypasses the arms allowlist and uses raw filesystem access — producing apparently-correct answers while measuring the wrong thing.\n\nSecondary impact: any downstream Orbit integration that wants to expose graph tools to a headless Claude Code process hits the same wall.\n\n## Suggested Fixes / Investigations\n\n1. Rename the benchmark mcp.json server from `orbit` to something that cant collide with the parents disabled entry (e.g. `orbit-bench`), then re-test. If this works, document that `--strict-mcp-config` does NOT bypass `disabledMcpServers` name-matching.\n2. Add a `--mcp-debug` log to the reproduction to capture the exact connection failure reason.\n3. Test whether a trivial echo-server declared via `--mcp-config` connects successfully, to isolate whether the issue is orbit-specific or affects any `--mcp-config` server in child sessions.\n4. If the root cause is `disabledMcpServers` shadowing, either rename on Orbits side (suggested fix in T20260422-1621s Claude Code bundle) or file a Claude Code bug.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Blocks benchmark harness execution in T20260422-1609 (graph-only and hybrid arms). `no-graph` arm works.\n- Reproduction is deterministic: every `claude -p ... --mcp-config benchmarks/graph/mcp.json --strict-mcp-config` invocation from this workspace hits the issue.\n- Verified 2026-04-22 on claude-code version 2.1.114, orbit 0.1.0.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nOnce Orbit has a first-class `orbit mcp init/remove` scaffold, the Codex-specific setup should live under `orbit mcp init --codex` instead of being described as direct `workspace init` config mutation. Codex needs provider-specific integration for the current workspace so Orbit can register its MCP server in project-local Codex config without users hand-authoring the server stanza themselves.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Codex should participate in the same MCP setup flow as Claude instead of being a one-off workspace-init side effect.\n- Project-local Codex config is the right integration boundary for this repo; Orbit can seed it without mutating the user's global `~/.codex/config.toml`.\n- This keeps the happy path coherent: `orbit workspace init` delegates to `orbit mcp init --auto`, and the Codex portion of that path is implemented here.\n\n## Scope\n\n1. **Codex seeder under `mcp init`** — implement the Codex-specific branch of `orbit mcp init --codex` so it creates or updates project-local `.codex/config.toml` with an Orbit MCP server entry for the current workspace.\n2. **Safe-default server registration** — the generated Codex config should point at the current supported Orbit MCP command (`orbit serve mcp`) and rely on the server's default safe surface (graph read + stable `orbit.task.*` tools).     "description": "## Problem\n\nOnce Orbit has a first-class `orbit mcp init/remove` scaffold, the Claude-specific setup should live under `orbit mcp init --claude` instead of a one-off `workspace init` bundle. Claude needs provider-specific integration for the current workspace so users do not have to hand-edit Claude MCP registration and project permissions before Orbit's graph and task tools become usable.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Claude should participate in the same MCP setup flow as Codex instead of having a special case hidden inside `workspace init`.\n- The provider-specific Claude work is still needed even after the server-side gate exists, because Claude requires client configuration for discoverability and permissions.\n- This keeps the UX coherent: `orbit workspace init` delegates to `orbit mcp init --auto`, and the Claude portion of that path is implemented here.\n\n## Scope\n\n1. **Claude seeder under `mcp init`** — implement the Claude-specific branch of `orbit mcp init --claude`, including the Claude MCP registration and the project-level permission/config entries needed for the current workspace.\n2. **Safe-default client exposure** — the generated Claude config should line up with the default safe Orbit MCP surface (graph read + stable `orbit.task.*` tools). Experimental graph write exposure remains server-gated via `orbit serve mcp --allow-write`; this task does not make write tools part of the default Claude setup.\n3. **Remove path** — implement the Claude-specific branch of `orbit mcp remove --claude` so Orbit-owned Claude integration can be removed cleanly without disturbing unrelated user config.\n4. **Workspace init delegation** — ensure the Claude seeder is what runs when `orbit workspace init` delegates to `orbit mcp init --auto` and Claude is selected.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Preserve unrelated Claude config and do not overwrite user-managed entries.\n- Never modify `.claude/settings.local.json`; that remains the user's private override layer.\n- Favor the current supported Orbit startup command (`orbit serve mcp`), not the removed `orbit mcp start` entrypoint.\n- This task depends on the shared MCP scaffold/gating task and should not re-invent command parsing or provider selection logic locally.",
