    "plan": "# Execution Plan\n\n## Phase 1 — Shared selector utility in `orbit-common`\n\n**Goal:** one source of truth for parsing, canonicalizing, anchoring, and overlap — consumed by every downstream site.\n\n1. Create `crates/orbit-common/src/selector.rs` (sibling to existing `paths.rs` and task helpers in `task.rs`).\n2. Define:\n   - `pub fn canonical_selector(input: &str, workspace: &Path) -> Result<String, SelectorError>` — accepts raw path, `<path>:<line>`, `<path>:<start>-<end>`, and already-canonical selectors; emits canonical selector string. Line-range inputs currently downgrade to `file:<path>` (symbol upgrade gated on T20260421-0358).\n   - `pub fn anchor_path(selector: &str) -> Result<PathBuf, SelectorError>` — extracts the filesystem anchor: `file:x` → `x`; `dir:x` → `x`; `symbol:x#foo:fn` → `x`.\n   - `pub fn exists_in_workspace(selector: &str, workspace: &Path) -> bool` — filesystem-only check on the anchor; does not touch the graph.\n   - `pub fn overlaps(a: &str, b: &str) -> bool` — implements the overlap rules in the ACs. Pure function over anchor paths + selector kinds.\n3. Reuse `orbit-knowledge::Selector` as the parser internally if the dependency graph permits; otherwise mirror the parse logic (selector syntax is small — three prefixes — so a local mirror is acceptable if it avoids a crate-layer violation).\n4. No graph access in this utility. Graph-backed symbol resolution lives in the normalizer (Phase 2), not here. Keeps this layer testable in isolation.\n\n## Phase 2 — Canonicalize on write (file/dir only, pre-T0358)\n\n**Goal:** `orbit.task.add` and `orbit.task.update` stop storing raw paths.\n\n1. In `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/task/add.rs` and `update.rs` (and in the underlying tool implementations in `crates/orbit-tools/src/task/task_add.rs:57` / `task_update.rs:81`), run each `context_files` entry through `canonical_selector`.\n2. Graph-unavailable branch: if the normalizer wants to upgrade `<path>:<line>` to a symbol but `KnowledgeStore::open` fails or the ref is missing, keep the entry as `file:<path>` with a stderr warning. One warning per invocation, not per entry.\n3. Make `orbit.task.add` accept either array-of-strings or comma-separated string for `context_files` — currently only `update` does. This is the papercut codex flagged; fix it while the code is open.\n4. Return the canonicalized list in the `orbit.task.add` / `orbit.task.update` response envelope so callers can see what got stored.\n5. Pruning code at `crates/orbit-tools/src/task/task.rs:469` stops treating entries as raw paths; it now operates on anchor paths via the shared utility.\n\n## Phase 3 — Lint is selector-aware\n\n**Goal:** eliminate T20260417-0350's false-positive class permanently.\n\n1. In `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/task/lint.rs:59`, replace the raw-path existence check with `exists_in_workspace` from the shared utility.\n2. The description-scan that currently looks for literal path-strings in the description should:\n   - Extract path-like tokens from the description (existing regex).\n   - For each token, strip a trailing `:<line>` / `:<line>:<col>` suffix.\n   - Compare against `anchor_path(entry)` for every `context_files` entry — a match or a containing-anchor satisfies the reference.\n   - Only if no anchor covers the token, do the existing filesystem check on the token itself.\n3. On graph miss: lint's selector validation degrades to anchor existence check; one warning.\n\n## Phase 4 — Conflict/lock logic uses anchors\n\n**Goal:** move every raw-string prefix match onto `overlaps()`.\n\n1. `crates/orbit-tools/src/parallel.rs:524` — replace prefix-match loop with a pairwise `overlaps()` call over anchor pairs.\n2. `crates/orbit-tools/src/task/mod.rs:584` (task locks) — same.\n3. `crates/orbit-store/src/task_reservation_store.rs:228` (reservations) — same.\n4. `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/commit.rs:264` (commit scoping) — same.\n5. Add a unit test per call site exercising the four canonical cases from the overlap AC.\n\n## Phase 5 — Agent-facing prompt/skill updates\n\n**Goal:** the instructions agents read match what the tools now do.\n\n1. `agents/**/agent_implement.yaml:30` (and any other implementer prompts): replace \"read each path in context_files\" with the selector-first instruction described in the AC (prefer `orbit.graph.pack`; fall back to `fs.read` only when selector is unresolved or graph is unavailable).\n2. Schema docstrings at `crates/orbit-tools/src/task/task_add.rs:57` and `task_update.rs:81`: describe the three selector forms and the raw-path acceptance + auto-upgrade behavior.\n3. `.claude/skills/orbit-create-task` and `.claude/skills/orbit-execute-task`: add a short \"Selector-first context\" section.\n\n## Phase 6 — Envelope read/write coherence\n\n**Goal:** no path that exits storage emits non-canonical `context_files`; no path that enters storage skips the normalizer.\n\n1. Audit `crates/orbit-tools/src/task/envelope.rs:182` — read path emits canonical form (idempotent: already-canonical entries pass through unchanged). Write path runs the normalizer.\n2. `orbit task prune-context` and any batch-refresh paths that write `context_files`: route through the normalizer.\n\n## Phase 7 — Symbol-phase, gated on T20260421-0358\n\n**Goal:** `<path>:<line>` upgrades to `symbol:<path>#<name>:<kind>` once per-branch refs exist.\n\n1. Add a guard in `canonical_selector`: when T0358 is merged and per-branch refs are available, attempt graph lookup for line-range inputs. Resolve to the tightest covering symbol node. Fall back to `file:<path>` if no symbol covers.\n2. Guard is a cargo feature or a runtime capability check — whichever keeps this task landable independently. Flip the switch in a follow-up commit once T0358 merges.\n\n## Phase 8 — Rewrite-on-touch migration\n\n**Goal:** no flag day. Old tasks keep working; new writes canonicalize.\n\n1. Field type unchanged (`Vec<String>`). No schema migration.\n2. `orbit.task.update` always runs the normalizer over `context_files`, even when the update doesn't explicitly touch that field (i.e. the normalizer is part of the read-modify-write cycle, not gated on the user having passed `context_files`).\n3. For explicit backfill: `orbit task prune-context --write` triggers the normalizer on every task without changing anything else.\n\n## Risks & Mitigations\n\n- **Risk:** `overlaps()` semantics miss a real conflict case we don't have a test for. **Mitigation:** enumerate the four canonical cases in the AC as unit tests; accept one round of post-landing refinement.\n- **Risk:** normalizer has a subtle bug that corrupts `context_files` during rewrite-on-touch. **Mitigation:** log-only mode for one release: normalize and diff, but don't write, until we've verified no diffs appear in practice. (Optional; decide at review time.)\n- **Risk:** graph-unavailable warning spam if a downstream tool loops over many entries. **Mitigation:** one warning per invocation, not per entry. Test explicitly.\n- **Risk:** T20260417-0350 is in flight and partially addresses Change 1. **Mitigation:** supersede T0350 explicitly; fold any of its in-progress work into Phase 3 rather than duplicating it.\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Changing `context_files` field type away from `Vec<String>`.\n- A new `context_selectors` field.\n- Cross-repo selectors.\n- Graph-level conflict semantics beyond \"same file, different symbols = overlap.\" (Finer granularity — non-overlapping symbol ranges in one file — is a follow-up; not worth the complexity before we see real contention.)\n",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nThe knowledge graph at `.orbit/knowledge/` has a single ref slot: `graph/refs/current.json`. This means:\n\n1. **Wrong-branch answers.** An agent working on `feature/x` running `orbit.graph.search` reads whichever branch's graph was built last. Results reflect stale branch state rather than the agent's actual code context.\n2. **Concurrent rebuild races.** Two worktrees rebuilding on different branches clobber each other's `current.json`.\n3. **Layering bug (T20260421-0331) is a symptom.** `KnowledgeStore::open` hardcodes `graph/refs/current.json` and does its own path arithmetic instead of delegating to `GraphObjectStore`. The single-ref model made this duplication invisible because there was only one path to compute.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nEvery graph tool (`orbit.graph.overview`, `search`, `show`, `refs`, `pack`) answers against whatever graph was written last. That is fine in a single-branch demo; it is actively misleading across branches. As agents operate increasingly across parallel branches and worktrees, wrong-branch answers become wrong-context reasoning.\n\nFixing this also unblocks deferred tasks T20260421-0342 and T20260421-0343 (symbol-level task history), since symbol identity is branch-scoped.\n\n## Design Direction\n\nMirror git's object/ref split, using git itself as the source of truth for \"which branch is active\":\n\n- **Object store stays shared and content-addressed.** Content-addressed objects already deduplicate across branches for free. No change to `objects/` layout.\n- **Refs become a namespace.** Replace `refs/current.json` with `refs/heads/<branch-name>.json`, one file per branch.\n- **No internal HEAD file.** Active ref is resolved by asking git (`git symbolic-ref --short HEAD`). Avoids a second source of truth that can drift from git.\n- **No `refs/worktrees/` namespace.** Each git worktree has a distinct branch checked out, so branch-keying already gives worktree isolation.\n- **Single resolver.** `KnowledgeStore::open` stops knowing the graph directory layout. It delegates to `GraphObjectStore`, which owns ref resolution. This subsumes the T20260421-0331 fix.\n- **Atomic ref writes.** Standard tempfile + rename, so a crash mid-rebuild can't produce a half-written ref.\n\n## Explicitly Out of Scope (follow-ups)\n\n- `orbit graph gc` for orphan object collection. Orphans are cheap disk; wait for pain.\n- `orbit graph list-refs` command. Add when a real caller needs it.\n- A separate `refs/worktrees/` namespace. Worktrees get isolation from branch-keying.\n- An internal `HEAD` file. Use git's HEAD directly.\n- Cross-repo ref sharing.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Code lives in `orbit-knowledge` (`graph/object_store.rs`, `store/open.rs`) and `orbit-cli` (graph command + tool wrappers).\n- Ref-path frame-of-reference must be documented exactly once, at the write site, and consumed consistently. Current code writes `\"index\": \"graph/index/by-id.json\"` (knowledge-root-relative) and then `validate_graph_index_ref` silently strips `graph/` — that asymmetry is part of the T20260421-0331 root cause and must be removed as part of this work.\n- Detached HEAD / no-branch case must produce a clear error, not a silent write to a placeholder ref.\n- Back-compat: one release cycle of auto-migration from `refs/current.json` to `refs/heads/<default-branch>.json`; after that, the legacy path is unsupported.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\nImplemented branch-aware knowledge-graph refs by replacing the single `graph/refs/current.json` slot with branch-scoped `graph/refs/heads/<branch>.json` refs that point at immutable per-build index files. Added shared git branch/default-branch resolution helpers, migrated `KnowledgeStore::open` and all graph read paths onto `GraphObjectStore` ref resolution, added optional `--ref` / `ref` plumbing to the CLI and `orbit.graph.*` read tools, and wired build persistence to atomically update only the branch ref after writing the new graph index. Added a tracked design note plus regression coverage for legacy migration, default-branch fallback, concurrent branch writes, and the `orbit.graph.pack` selector from T20260421-0331.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Immutable per-build index files instead of a shared `graph/index/by-id.json` slot | Rationale: per-branch refs alone would still race on a shared mutable index file.\n- Explicit `ref` reads do not auto-rebuild from the current checkout or overlay task-local working-graph edits | Rationale: an explicit ref should read stored graph state, not silently fabricate that ref from whatever branch is currently checked out.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- Integration tests were added but not executed in this turn because repo instructions forbid running tests | Severity: Medium | Mitigation: the scenarios are encoded in `crates/orbit-knowledge/tests/branch_refs.rs` for the next validation pass.\n\n## Recommended Follow-Ups\n- Run the new integration coverage in a later pass when test execution is permitted, especially the repo-wide pack smoke and concurrent-write cases.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe feature now uses a single branch-aware resolver path for reads, avoids cross-branch ref/index clobbering, preserves a default-branch fallback for read-only callers, and passes the repo-required `make fmt` and `make build` checks.\n\n---\n\n# Knowledge Graph Refs\n\nOrbit stores knowledge-graph objects in a shared content-addressed store under `.orbit/knowledge/graph/objects/` and `.orbit/knowledge/graph/blobs/`, while branch selection lives in branch-scoped ref files under `.orbit/knowledge/graph/refs/heads/<branch>.json`. Each ref points at an immutable per-build index file such as `.orbit/knowledge/graph/index/by-id/<root-graph-hash>.json`, so concurrent worktrees can rebuild different branches without clobbering each other's active graph view.\n\nLegacy repositories may still contain `.orbit/knowledge/graph/refs/current.json`. On the first open/write that can resolve the repo default branch, Orbit migrates that legacy file to `.orbit/knowledge/graph/refs/heads/<default-branch>.json` and removes the legacy path. Reads default to the current git branch, fall back to the default branch when the current branch has no graph ref yet, and writes never fall back.\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n    Git[\"Git branch selection\"] --> Ref[\"refs/heads/<branch>.json\"]\n    Ref --> Index[\"index/by-id/<root-graph-hash>.json\"]\n    Index --> Objects[\"objects/<hh>/<hash>.json\"]\n    Index --> Blobs[\"blobs/<hh>/<hash>.txt\"]\n```\n",

    "plan": "# Execution Plan\n\n## Phase 1 — Ref namespace foundation\n\n**Goal:** introduce `refs/heads/<branch>.json` layout alongside existing `refs/current.json`. No behavior change yet.\n\n1. In `orbit-knowledge/src/graph/object_store.rs`, add a `RefName` newtype (wraps `String`, validates against git ref-name rules: no `..`, no leading `-`, no whitespace, etc.). Reject absolute paths and traversal.\n2. Add methods on `GraphObjectStore`:\n   - `fn ref_path(&self, name: &RefName) -> PathBuf` → `self.graph_dir.join(\"refs/heads\").join(format!(\"{name}.json\"))`\n   - `fn read_ref(&self, name: &RefName) -> Result<CurrentRef, KnowledgeError>`\n   - `fn write_ref_atomic(&self, name: &RefName, current_ref: &CurrentRef) -> Result<(), KnowledgeError>` — writes to `<path>.tmp`, fsyncs, renames.\n   - `fn resolve_ref(&self, name: &RefName) -> Result<PathBuf, KnowledgeError>` — returns resolved index path (single resolver).\n3. Do NOT remove `current.json` support yet. Leave `read_graph()` pointing at the legacy ref for now.\n\n## Phase 2 — Single resolver, T20260421-0331 fix\n\n**Goal:** eliminate duplicated path logic in `KnowledgeStore::open`. Fix the stripping bug as a side effect.\n\n1. Change `KnowledgeStore::open(knowledge_dir: &Path)` signature to `open(knowledge_dir: &Path, ref_name: Option<&RefName>)`.\n2. Inside `open`, construct `GraphObjectStore::new(knowledge_dir.join(\"graph\"))` and call its `resolve_ref` method. No manual `graph/refs/current.json` string. No manual `join`+`strip_prefix` dance.\n3. Remove the `strip_prefix(\"graph/\")` normalization from `validate_graph_index_ref`. Rename it (e.g. `validate_relative_path`) or inline it into `GraphObjectStore`. The `index` field in `current.json` is documented as knowledge-root-relative at the write site (line with `\"index\": \"graph/index/by-id.json\"`); readers that have a `graph_dir` rather than `knowledge_dir` strip the known prefix explicitly and locally.\n4. Update all call sites of `KnowledgeStore::open` to pass a ref name (Phase 3 wires actual branch resolution; for this phase, pass a \"default\" ref name constant that resolves to the legacy current.json behavior via a shim).\n5. Add a regression test: `GraphObjectStore::write` → `KnowledgeStore::open` round-trips with a temp dir. This test would have caught T20260421-0331.\n\n## Phase 3 — Branch-aware reads/writes\n\n**Goal:** every graph call resolves against the current git branch.\n\n1. Add a thin helper in `orbit-common` (or reuse an existing git helper if present): `current_branch(workspace: &Path) -> Result<Option<String>, OrbitError>`. Shells out to `git symbolic-ref --short HEAD` or uses an existing git crate already in the dep tree. Returns `None` on detached HEAD.\n2. `orbit graph build` (in `orbit-cli`):\n   - Accepts `--ref <name>` (optional).\n   - When omitted, resolves to `current_branch`. On detached HEAD or no branch, exits non-zero with the error described in the ACs.\n   - Writes the ref via `GraphObjectStore::write_ref_atomic`.\n3. All `orbit.graph.*` tool wrappers (`OrbitKnowledgeOverviewTool`, `OrbitKnowledgeSearchTool`, etc.) accept an optional `ref` parameter. When omitted, resolve via `current_branch`. Document the parameter in each tool's schema.\n4. Read fallback: if `refs/heads/<current>.json` is absent, attempt `refs/heads/<default-branch>.json`. Default branch is resolved via `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` or, failing that, checking for `main`/`master` in that order. Emit a single stderr warning line; never fall back on writes.\n\n## Phase 4 — Migration + back-compat\n\n**Goal:** existing repos with `refs/current.json` migrate without manual intervention.\n\n1. On `GraphObjectStore::open`-equivalent (or first use), detect legacy layout (`refs/current.json` exists AND `refs/heads/` is absent or empty).\n2. Resolve the default branch. Rename the legacy file to `refs/heads/<default-branch>.json`. Delete the legacy file (the rename replaces it; no extra delete needed). Log one INFO line: `migrated knowledge graph ref: refs/current.json -> refs/heads/<name>.json`.\n3. If both `refs/current.json` and `refs/heads/<default-branch>.json` exist (unexpected but possible from a partial migration), prefer the `heads/` file and log a WARN naming both paths.\n4. Mark `refs/current.json` support as deprecated in CHANGELOG / release notes. After one release, remove the migration path.\n\n## Phase 5 — Concurrency & integration test\n\n**Goal:** verify two worktrees can build concurrently without corruption.\n\n1. Write an integration test (under `crates/orbit-knowledge/tests/` or equivalent) that:\n   - Sets up two tempdir \"worktrees\" that share an object store directory (symlinked or via a pointer — whichever matches real multi-worktree layout).\n   - Kicks off two `GraphObjectStore::write` + `write_ref_atomic` operations on different branch names concurrently.\n   - Asserts: both ref files parse, both reference reachable objects, no object file is corrupted, no ref file is truncated.\n2. Verify atomic-rename semantics on the target filesystems (APFS, ext4). No special handling beyond standard rename; document if any platform needs tweaking.\n\n## Phase 6 — Cleanup & docs\n\n1. Update `docs/design/` with the new ref layout (one paragraph + one diagram is enough).\n2. Update `CLAUDE.md`'s Orbit crate architecture note if the knowledge layout description changes.\n3. Announce deprecation of `refs/current.json`.\n\n## Risks & Mitigations\n\n- **Risk:** git invocation in hot paths adds latency. **Mitigation:** cache `current_branch()` per-process; invalidate on explicit `--ref` override.\n- **Risk:** `git symbolic-ref` returns unexpected values on submodules or non-repo paths. **Mitigation:** treat any non-zero exit as \"no current branch\" and surface the error clearly.\n- **Risk:** ref name validation differs subtly from git's. **Mitigation:** use git's own rules (see `git-check-ref-format(1)`), or run `git check-ref-format` when in doubt; add a test suite of edge cases (slashes, dots, unicode).\n- **Risk:** migration races if two processes open the store simultaneously during upgrade. **Mitigation:** the rename is atomic; worst case is one process sees the legacy layout and the other the new, both succeed.\n",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nThe on-demand `orbit task history <symbol>` command (see `T20260421-0342`) proves the UX but has two known weaknesses: it is slow on repos with deep history, and it loses the trail when symbols are renamed or moved. Agents working on hot paths will pay this cost repeatedly.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nIf symbol-level history lookup is useful (to be validated by the on-demand prototype first), agents will call it often — per-file, per-function, during code reading. At that frequency, a `git log -L` walk per call is wasteful, and losing history across renames hides exactly the refactor context that is most valuable.\n\nAn indexed `task_id → symbol` edge set in the knowledge graph turns every lookup into a graph query and — if keyed on symbol identity rather than line range — survives renames.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- **Dependency:** do not start until `T20260421-0342` is merged and has seen at least some usage; the indexed form should mirror the on-demand tool's output shape exactly so callers are portable.\n- **Where the edges live:** extend the knowledge graph schema in `orbit-knowledge` with a `touched_by` (or similar) edge: symbol node → task node, with attributes `{commit_sha, commit_date}`.\n- **Backfill pass:** a one-shot command (`orbit task history reindex` or similar) walks `git log` across the repo, parses task IDs out of commit messages, maps each commit's changed hunks to symbol ranges at that commit's tree, and writes edges. The backfill must be idempotent.\n- **Incremental maintenance:** a post-commit mechanism (git hook installed by `orbit init`, or an opt-in `orbit task index --since HEAD~1` command) updates the index after each tagged commit. Do not rely on a `post-commit` hook being the only path — provide an explicit CLI command so CI and recovery workflows work.\n- **Rename survival:** when a symbol moves or is renamed, use `git log --follow` plus graph-level symbol identity (stable ID if the graph already assigns one; otherwise fall back to qualified_name plus signature_hash) so edges persist across the rename rather than orphaning.\n- **Switch the lookup command:** `orbit task history <symbol>` should prefer the index when present and fall back to the on-demand `git log -L` walk when the index is missing or the symbol has no indexed edges. Output must remain identical.\n- **Staleness signal:** the command should warn on stderr when the index is older than HEAD by more than N commits (configurable, default 50).\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Cross-repository lookup.\n- Indexing commits that do not carry [T...] tags.\n- Re-deriving task artifacts when they are missing.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nOrbit commits carry `[T<task-id>]` tags in their messages, but agents have no ergonomic way to ask \"which tasks shaped this symbol?\". Reading entire files of history is too coarse — agents need symbol-granular lookup that joins git history to Orbit task artifacts (purpose, plan, review).\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nTask artifacts contain rich context (why a change was made, constraints, review notes) that is invisible from the code alone. Without a lookup tool, agents either guess or ignore this context. A thin on-demand wrapper proves the UX before we invest in an index.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- This task implements the **on-demand** variant only. A follow-up task will cover the indexed/hooked version for performance and rename survival.\n- Resolve the symbol to `file:startLine-endLine` via the existing knowledge graph (`orbit-knowledge` / `orbit.graph.*` tools).\n- Use `git log -L <startLine>,<endLine>:<file>` to enumerate commits touching the symbol range.\n- Extract task IDs by regex matching `\\[T\\d{8}-\\d{4}(?:-\\d+)?\\]` in commit messages.\n- Join task IDs to Orbit task artifacts via existing task store APIs; skip IDs that do not resolve (commit may predate the task system).\n- Output ordering: most recent commit first.\n- Command lives under `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/task/` as a new subcommand (`orbit task history`). Follow the existing subcommand pattern (see `list.rs`, `show.rs`).\n- `--depth` flag: `summary` (default, returns `task_id + purpose + commit_sha + commit_date`) and `full` (adds plan excerpt, review notes, linked PR if present).\n- Behavior when symbol cannot be resolved in the graph: exit non-zero with a clear error naming the symbol and suggesting `orbit graph search`.\n- Behavior when graph resolves but no tagged commits are found: exit zero, print an empty result with a note (`no tagged commits touched <symbol>`).\n\n## Out of Scope\n\n- Maintaining any index, hook, or cache.\n- Cross-repository lookup.\n- Rename tracking beyond what `git log -L` provides natively.",

    "plan": "## Investigation\n\nThe reported bug was real at filing time (2026-04-21 03:31 UTC) but was **incidentally fixed** ~30 minutes later by commit `6a573d18` (T20260421-0358 \"Scope graph refs by branch\"), which restructured graph index resolution.\n\n### Original (broken) resolution — before 6a573d18\n\n`KnowledgeStore::open` (old `crates/orbit-knowledge/src/store/open.rs`) did:\n\n```rust\nlet graph_index_path = knowledge_dir.join(validate_graph_index_ref(&current_ref.index)?);\n```\n\nWhere `validate_graph_index_ref` stripped the `graph/` prefix:\n\n```rust\nlet index_rel = index_ref.strip_prefix(\"graph/\").unwrap_or(index_ref);\n```\n\nNet effect: `current_ref.index = \"graph/index/by-id/<hash>.json\"` → stripped to `\"index/by-id/<hash>.json\"` → joined onto `knowledge_dir` → resolved to `.orbit/knowledge/index/by-id/<hash>.json` (wrong — missing `graph/`).\n\n### Current (fixed) resolution — after 6a573d18\n\n- `KnowledgeStore::open` now constructs a `GraphObjectStore` anchored at `knowledge_dir/graph` (see [open.rs:36](crates/orbit-knowledge/src/store/open.rs:36)).\n- The index lookup flows through `GraphObjectStore::resolve_index_path` ([object_store.rs:272-285](crates/orbit-knowledge/src/graph/object_store.rs:272)), which strips `graph` and joins onto `self.graph_dir` (already `knowledge_dir/graph`). Result: `.orbit/knowledge/graph/index/by-id/<hash>.json` — correct.\n- `validate_graph_index_ref` has been deleted; `resolve_index_path` is the single resolution site.\n\n### Existing regression coverage\n\n`pack_regression_selector_opens_from_branch_ref_layout` at [crates/orbit-knowledge/tests/branch_refs.rs:298-328](crates/orbit-knowledge/tests/branch_refs.rs:298) already uses **the exact selector** from this task description (`symbol:crates/orbit-cli/src/command/graph.rs#GraphSearchArgs::execute:method`), builds a fresh knowledge graph, opens it via `KnowledgeStore::open`, calls `store.pack(...)`, and asserts one resolved node with zero unresolved selectors.     "description": "## Problem\nOrbit tasks currently have no way to express \"task X cannot proceed until task Y is done.\" In practice we resolve this by writing the dependency graph into the commit messages or summary tables of other tasks (see the Groundhog G1-G5 / G-V breakdown). That is fragile — the graph is invisible to the task list, cannot be queried for ready work, and is not validated against renames or deletions.\n\nAdd a first-class `dependencies` field to task records.\n\n## Why It Matters\n- **Ready-queue filtering.** Agents picking up backlog work need to know which tasks are actually startable (all dependencies `done` or `approved`) vs. which are blocked waiting on prerequisites. Without a field, every pickup requires the agent to reason about cross-task ordering from prose.\n- **Dependency integrity.** Referenced task IDs should be validated at write-time so deletions/renames surface immediately rather than leaving dangling references in descriptions.\n- **Observability.** `orbit.task.show` should display the dependency chain and each dependency's current status, enabling quick \"why is this blocked\" diagnosis.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- New optional field on the task record: `dependencies: Vec<TaskId>`.\n- Backward compatible: existing task files without the field must load/save as if `dependencies: []`.\n- Validation happens in `orbit.task.add` and `orbit.task.update`:\n  - Each referenced id must resolve to an existing task in the same scope (WorkspaceOnly per the scoping table in `CLAUDE.md`).\n  - Direct cycles (A → B → A) and indirect cycles (A → B → C → A) must be rejected with an error naming the cycle participants.\n  - Self-reference (A → A) rejected.\n- `orbit.task.show` output includes the `dependencies` list with each entry's current status resolved inline (e.g. `T20260420-0509 [done]`).\n- `orbit.task.list` gains a `ready: bool` filter: when `true`, returns only tasks whose `dependencies` are all in terminal-accepted states (`done`, `approved`). Ignored/irrelevant for tasks with empty `dependencies`.\n- `orbit.task.start` (backlog → in-progress): if any dependency is not in a terminal-accepted state, emit a descriptive warning naming the unmet dependency but **do not block** the transition. Agents may legitimately race a dependency's approval. Blocking is out of scope for v1.\n- Storage lives in `orbit-store` task records; surface command wiring lives in `orbit-tools`.\n- No new reverse-index storage: a `dependents` relation, if displayed, is computed at read time by scanning the task set.\n\n## Out of Scope (v1)\n- Auto-notification when a dependency transitions to `done`.\n- Graph visualisation / rendering.\n- Hard-blocking `orbit.task.start` when dependencies are unmet (see constraint above).",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Added first-class `dependencies` storage to Orbit task records and update flows so task YAML bundles load missing fields as `[]` and persist explicit dependency IDs.\n- Added shared dependency helpers for normalization, readiness checks, unresolved-ID validation, self-dependency rejection, and cycle detection.\n- Exposed dependency-aware task output across the Orbit tool host, direct CLI, and dashboard/API JSON surfaces via raw `dependencies` plus inline `resolved_dependencies`.\n- Added `ready` filtering to task listing and emitted non-blocking start warnings when dependencies are not yet satisfied.\n- Synced direct CLI task add/update/list/show surfaces and Orbit builtin tool schemas to the new dependency inputs/outputs.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe implementation builds cleanly, formats cleanly, and the freshly built workspace binary smoke-checks the new dependency-aware `orbit.task.show` and `orbit.task.list` output shapes.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- The globally installed `orbit` binary is separate from the workspace build output, so smoke verification used `./target/debug/orbit` rather than `~/.cargo/bin/orbit`.\n- The task requested a round-trip parse/render test, but repo instructions explicitly say `DO NOT WRITE UNIT TESTS or RUN TESTS`, so that acceptance item remains unimplemented.\n\n## Deviations from Original Plan\n- Omitted the requested output round-trip test because the workspace instructions prohibit adding or running tests.",

    "description": "## Problem\nWire G1-G4 and the G-V verifier into a runnable Groundhog v1 activity. The runner manages the full checkpoint loop: read the next checkpoint, snapshot the workspace, run an HTTP-backend agent attempt, dispatch the terminal verb, verify success mechanically, rewind-or-advance, and persist prompt-facing success memory plus runner state.\n\n## Why It Matters\nThis is the first end-to-end Groundhog execution path. It proves the narrow v1 model before deviation stacks, critics, or cache-specific optimizations are layered on top.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- New activity kind `groundhog`; do not overload `agent_loop` for v1.\n- Backend is fixed to `http` (Groundhog v1 is HTTP-only).\n- Loop shape:\n  1. Read the plan's next checkpoint.\n  2. `WorkspaceSnapshot::create` starts the day.\n  3. Invoke the HTTP-loop agent until it emits `checkpoint_success` or `checkpoint_failure`.\n  4. On `checkpoint_success`: run the mechanical verifier. All pass -> `commit_success`, append successful day memory, advance. Any fail -> synthesize a failure report and reuse the failure path.\n  5. On `checkpoint_failure`: `rewind`, increment attempt count, and retry while budget remains.\n  6. Budget exhausted: persist the checkpoint as abandoned and mark the task blocked; do not introduce deviation behavior under the v1 banner.\n- Chronicle/state persist to task artifacts on every day close.\n- Authoritative spec: `docs/design/groundhogv1.md`.\n\n## Dependencies\n- G1, G2, G3, G4, G-V\n",
    "execution_summary": "Implemented the Groundhog v1 activity runner around a new `groundhog` v2 activity kind. The change wires Groundhog-aware tool context through the runtime, adds dispatcher and CLI support for the new activity type, and introduces a runner that loads task checkpoints, creates git-backed workspace snapshots, runs fresh HTTP agent attempts, handles `checkpoint_success` / `checkpoint_failure`, verifies mechanical success criteria, rewinds on failure, commits successful checkpoint work, and persists chronicle plus runner-state artifacts across retries. `make fmt`, `make build`, and `orbit.task.lint` all completed successfully. Scope was also finalized to the v1 design in docs/design/groundhogv1.md, so deviation-stack behavior and broader post-v1 coverage remain follow-up work rather than hidden requirements in this task.",

    "plan": "## V1 Scope Freeze\n\n- Canonical design spec for this task is `docs/design/groundhogv1.md`.\n- Treat Groundhog v1 as HTTP-only, checkpoint-driven, and success/failure-only.\n- Do **not** quietly expand this task to include executor-authored deviation stacks, critic-on-retry, chronicle compaction, or provider-specific cache requirements.\n- The current task metadata still mentions deviation handling and an integration test. Until the human widens scope, treat those as follow-up / re-scope items rather than hidden work inside this task.\n\n## File Targets\n\n- `docs/design/groundhogv1.md`\n- `crates/orbit-common/src/groundhog.rs`\n- `crates/orbit-common/src/types/task_plan.rs`\n- `crates/orbit-common/src/types/activity_job/activity_v2.rs` and adjacent v2 activity type surfaces\n- `crates/orbit-engine/src/activity_job/`\n- `crates/orbit-engine/src/workspace_snapshot.rs`\n- `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/v2_host.rs`\n- `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/`\n\n## Implementation Steps\n\n1. Canonicalize the spec and entry point.\n   - Point Groundhog-related code comments and task context at `docs/design/groundhogv1.md` or add a deliberate compatibility shim if `groundhog.md` must remain.\n   - Decide whether Groundhog lands as a new v2 activity kind or the smallest equivalent wrapper around the existing HTTP agent-loop path; document that choice in code.\n\n2. Add a dedicated Groundhog runner in `orbit-engine`.\n   - Own checkpoint iteration, attempt counters, day numbering, task-artifact writes, and workspace snapshot lifecycle.\n   - Keep prompt-facing success memory separate from audit-only attempt history.\n\n3. Build per-attempt prompt assembly and HTTP dispatch.\n   - Assemble prompt context in this order: system/tools/skills, structured task plan, successful checkpoint summaries, current checkpoint spec, latest failure report.\n   - Reuse the existing HTTP loop driver for a fresh session per attempt; never carry scratch transcripts across attempts.\n\n4. Wire the Groundhog verb surface through `ToolContext`.\n   - Install a Groundhog host across the engine/runtime boundary so `checkpoint_success`, `checkpoint_failure`, and `side_effect` resolve structurally.\n   - Success path captures summary + side effects, then hands off to the verifier.\n   - Failure path persists `FailureReport`, rewinds the workspace, and advances retry state.\n\n5. Implement the mechanical verifier gate.\n   - Evaluate `command`, `file_exists`, and `file_contains` criteria out-of-band from the model loop.\n   - Treat verifier failure as a synthesized `checkpoint_failure`, with verifier output persisted to audit artifacts only.\n   - Leave semantic criteria agent-judged in v1.\n\n6. Persist v1 state to task artifacts.\n   - Prompt-facing memory: successful checkpoint summaries plus durable side-effect summaries.\n   - Audit record: attempts, verifier runs, scratch refs, failure reports, and terminal checkpoint outcome.\n   - Persist on every attempt close so a session crash can restart from the last closed attempt.\n\n7. Define v1 terminal-state behavior.\n   - Retry within each checkpoint's attempt budget.\n   - On budget exhaustion, close the checkpoint as `abandoned` or `blocked` based on runtime conditions; do not introduce deviation behavior under the v1 banner.\n\n8. Cleanup and documentation.\n   - Keep task/code references aligned with v1 terminology.\n   - Leave deviation, critic, and cache-compaction work for follow-up tasks after the v1 loop is proven.\n\n## Validation\n\n- `make fmt`\n- `make build`\n- `orbit tool run --root /Users/daniel/workspace/repos/orbit/.orbit orbit.task.lint --input '{\"id\":\"T20260420-0510-2\",\"agent\":\"codex\",\"model\":\"gpt-5.4\"}'`\n- Do not add or run unit tests. If existing smoke/example coverage can be reused without creating a new test obligation, capture that separately instead of broadening this task.\n",

    "description": "## Problem\nWhen the agent emits `checkpoint_success`, the runtime must verify the checkpoint's mechanical success criteria (`command` exit code, `file_exists`, `file_contains`) without sending their output back through the agent's context. Semantic criteria are agent-judged and the verifier records them as skipped.\n\n## Why It Matters\nMechanical checks in-agent are wasteful: large build/test logs pollute the scratch transcript, bloat the cache tail, and burn tokens on deterministic pass/fail decisions. Moving mechanical verification to the runtime keeps the agent focused on code-writing and preserves cache economics per §3.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- Runs independent criteria in parallel where possible.\n- Supports criterion kinds from G2: `command` (exit code compared to `expect_exit`), `file_exists`, `file_contains` (regex), `semantic` (recorded as `skipped_semantic`).\n- On any mechanical failure: synthesize a `FailureReport` (type from G1) naming the failing criterion and including captured output truncated to a configurable cap (default 4 KB).\n- Failure flips the emitted verb to `checkpoint_failure` semantically — the day is treated as failed, retry is triggered per §7 (wiring of that decision belongs to G5, not this task).\n- Full verifier output persists to `artifacts.days[n].verifier_runs[*]` for post-hoc debugging.\n- Lives in `orbit-engine`.\n- Authoritative spec: `docs/design/groundhog.md` §8.7.\n\n## Dependencies\n- G1 (Chronicle / `FailureReport` types)\n- G2 (criterion schema)",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Added `crates/orbit-engine/src/checkpoint_verifier.rs` with a public `verify_checkpoint(criteria: &[Criterion], workspace: &Path) -> VerifierResult` surface, configurable output capping, parallel criterion evaluation, and `FailureReport` synthesis for mechanical failures.\n- Exported the verifier API from `orbit-engine` and added integration coverage in `crates/orbit-engine/tests/checkpoint_verifier.rs` for file criteria, semantic skipping, stderr capture, configurable truncation, and the parallel `sleep 1` command case.\n- Updated the task context to concrete Groundhog source files and filed friction task `T20260420-0540` for the missing `docs/design/groundhog.md` spec path referenced by active Groundhog tasks.\n- Merged the implementation into `agent-main`.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe runtime verifier behavior described in the task is implemented, `cargo build -p orbit-engine --tests` compiles the new test target, and both `make fmt` and `make build` pass.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- Integration tests were added but not executed. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: Repository instructions explicitly forbid running tests, so validation was limited to compiling the test target plus `make build`.\n\n## Recommended Follow-Ups\n- Wire `VerifierResult` into the future Groundhog runner so `verifier_runs` are persisted into task artifacts and `checkpoint_failure` retries can consume the synthesized `FailureReport` directly.",

    "description": "## Problem\nGroundhog days need reversible workspace state. On day-end failure, the workspace must rewind to the day-start snapshot. On success, the day's changes squash into the task branch.\n\n## Why It Matters\nWithout reliable rewind, failed attempts leak partial mutations into the next day's workspace, and the reset-to-clean-state semantic that makes retry meaningful is compromised.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- Snapshot mechanism: `git commit` onto a scratch branch `groundhog/<task_id>/day-<n>` (not `git stash`, for the reasons in §5.1).\n- Rewind: `git reset --hard <snapshot_ref>` on the task branch.\n- Success: squash scratch-branch commits into a single commit on the task branch; drop the scratch branch.\n- Must tolerate no-op days (a day that makes no edits should not error on snapshot or commit).\n- Must NOT touch untracked files outside the expected workspace and must NOT run `git clean -fdx`.\n- Rewind is guaranteed only for git-tracked state (§5.4); non-git side effects are out of scope for this task.\n- Lives in `orbit-engine` alongside other activity-execution mechanics.\n- Authoritative spec: `docs/design/groundhog.md` §5.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Added a task-branch movement guard to `WorkspaceSnapshot::rewind` and `WorkspaceSnapshot::commit_success`; both now abort with a descriptive manual-intervention error if the task branch head moved away from the saved snapshot during the Groundhog day.\n- Documented the exclusive task-branch ownership assumption directly on the public `WorkspaceSnapshot` API.\n- Expanded integration coverage with tests for pre-existing untracked-file preservation and the moved-task-branch guard, alongside the earlier rewind/squash/no-op/collision cases.\n- Made the scratch capture commit message more descriptive by including the task branch, snapshot short SHA, and a timestamp for post-hoc inspection.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe Groundhog workspace snapshot flow now protects against silent task-branch clobbering, covers the untracked-file preservation edge, and still passes `make fmt` plus `make build`.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- The integration tests were added but not run because project instructions forbid agent-run tests. | Severity: Medium | Mitigation: a human can run `cargo test -p orbit-engine --test workspace_snapshot` before approval.\n- No git commit was created because `CLAUDE.md` says not to commit until the human explicitly approves the task. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: if you approve this task, I can create the required `[T20260420-0509-4] ...` commit next.",

    "description": "## Problem\nThe Groundhog agent's entire decision surface reduces to four verbs. Each verb is implemented as a tool registered with orbit-tools so the HTTP-loop agent can invoke it from a normal `tool_use` block.\n\n## Why It Matters\nWithout these tools, the agent has no way to terminate a day or push a deviation. They are the sole interface between the agent and the Groundhog runner.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- Tools register via the standard `orbit-tools` registry.\n- Depends on G1 (Chronicle types) for input schemas.\n- Tool names: `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_success`, `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_failure`, `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_deviate`, `orbit.groundhog.side_effect`.\n- Tools publish the verb intent; the actual day-close / dispatch logic lives in the runner (G5). Tool handlers should fail fast with a descriptive error when invoked outside a Groundhog runner context.\n- Authoritative spec: `docs/design/groundhog.md` §2.5, §8.4.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\nAdded four new Orbit builtins under `crates/orbit-tools/src/builtin/orbit/`: `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_success`, `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_failure`, `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_deviate`, and `orbit.groundhog.side_effect`. Each tool is registered in the Orbit builtin registry, exposes explicit required parameters through `ToolSchema`, validates its top-level input fields before parsing, and reuses shared Groundhog / task-plan types (`SideEffect`, `FailureReport`, `TaskPlanCheckpoint`) instead of introducing duplicate request shapes.\n\nExtended `ToolContext` with an optional `GroundhogToolHost` seam plus `GroundhogBuiltinAction` / `GroundhogScope`, so the verb tools have a clean future hook for G5 while still failing fast today when no Groundhog runner context is active. The handlers intentionally validate input first, then return a descriptive `groundhog` execution error when invoked outside an active Groundhog day.\n\nValidated the implementation with `make fmt`, `make build`, a local `./target/debug/orbit ... tool list` check showing all four tool names, and two direct command checks: `{}` against `orbit.groundhog.checkpoint_success` now reports the missing required fields, while a valid `orbit.groundhog.side_effect` payload fails with a `groundhog` context error as expected outside the runner.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Added an optional Groundhog host abstraction in `ToolContext` now instead of waiting for G5. | Rationale: it keeps the G3 tools from becoming dead-end stubs and gives the eventual runner a narrow, explicit integration seam.\n- Reused `TaskPlanCheckpoint` for `checkpoint_deviate` input. | Rationale: the repo already has one authoritative checkpoint schema in `orbit-common`, so duplicating it here would create avoidable drift.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- The Groundhog tools still only support the error path until G5 wires a real runner host. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: the new host abstraction localizes that follow-up, and the current behavior is intentionally explicit and acceptance-compliant.\n- Tests were not run. | Severity: Medium | Mitigation: this repo instructs agents not to run tests, so validation stayed at `make fmt`, `make build`, and targeted CLI checks.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe Groundhog four-verb tool surface now exists in the registry with shared typed inputs, clear schema-driven validation, and attributable `groundhog` context failures, which is the right integration foundation for the upcoming runner work.",

    "description": "## Problem\nTask plans today are unstructured markdown/text. Groundhog execution requires a structured `checkpoints[]` array in the plan, with each checkpoint carrying specs, typed success criteria (mechanical vs. semantic), and attempt budgets.\n\n## Why It Matters\nPlan quality is load-bearing for Groundhog (see §10.1). Typed criteria are what let the runtime verifier (G-V) run mechanical checks outside the model loop; without the schema there is no way to distinguish mechanical from semantic criteria.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- Extend the task plan schema to support an optional top-level `checkpoints` array.\n- Each checkpoint: `id` (string, unique within plan), `spec` (string), `success_criteria` (array), `attempt_budget` (optional integer, default 3).\n- Each criterion has `kind: command | file_exists | file_contains | semantic` plus kind-specific fields (see §8.7 for the schema).\n- Backward compatible: plans without `checkpoints` must still parse into existing plan type.\n- Loader lives wherever task plans parse today (likely orbit-engine or orbit-tools — consult existing parser before placing).\n- Authoritative spec: `docs/design/groundhog.md` §4, §8.2, §8.7.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Added shared `TaskPlan`, `TaskPlanCheckpoint`, and `TaskPlanSuccessCriterion` types in `crates/orbit-common` for Groundhog-style structured checkpoints.\n- Implemented `parse_task_plan` / `TaskPlan::parse` to accept YAML task plans with optional top-level `checkpoints`, while treating legacy non-YAML markdown/text plans as backward-compatible empty structured plans.\n- Added validation for duplicate checkpoint ids plus kind-specific required-field errors for `command`, `file_exists`, `file_contains`, and `semantic` success criteria.\n- Added `TaskPlan::to_yaml_string` and `Task::parsed_plan()` so downstream runtime work can round-trip and consume `task.plan` through one shared parser surface.\n- Verified the change with `make fmt` and `make build`.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe shared schema/parser surface for Groundhog checkpoints is in place and compiles cleanly across the workspace.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- Structured-plan detection uses a conservative first-meaningful-line heuristic to distinguish YAML mappings from legacy markdown/text. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: tighten the caller contract or add an explicit schema marker once Groundhog execution paths are wired.\n\n## Deviations from Original Plan\n- No round-trip unit test was added. | Justification: repository instructions explicitly forbid writing or running unit tests, so the implementation exposes round-trip-capable serialization/parsing APIs but validates only through `make fmt` and `make build`.\n",

    "description": "## Problem\nGroundhog's runtime needs core data structures (`Chronicle`, `Day`, `Attempt`, `FailureReport`, `SideEffect`, `DayOutcome`) to track checkpoint execution lineage. The serializer must satisfy an append-only prefix invariant so prompt-cache breakpoints stay valid across day boundaries.\n\n## Why It Matters\nEvery later Groundhog component (runner, tools, verifier, critic) depends on this shape. The prefix invariant is load-bearing for cache economics — violating it invalidates `cache_control` breakpoints 3 and 4 on every retry, blowing the cache benefit that motivates the entire design.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n- Types live in a new module (proposed: `orbit-common::groundhog`); follow existing `orbit-common::types` patterns.\n- Canonical serialization: sorted map keys, no clock-dependent fields in serialized body, NFC-normalized strings.\n- `DayOutcome` includes `Abandoned { reason }` and `DeviatedTo(CheckpointId)` to represent logical removals without mutating prior bytes.\n- Persistence target: task artifact `artifacts.chronicle` via existing task.update artifact channel — no new store.\n- Authoritative spec: `docs/design/groundhog.md` §2.3, §4, §5.5.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\nAdded a new public `orbit_common::groundhog` module with the core Groundhog lineage types (`Chronicle`, `Day`, `Attempt`, `FailureReport`, `SideEffect`, `DayOutcome`) plus supporting `CheckpointId`, `Timestamp`, `ToolCallRecord`, and `SideEffectKind` definitions.\n\nImplemented `Chronicle::serialize_at(day_idx)` as a canonical line-oriented JSON record stream so earlier serializations remain byte-exact prefixes of later ones, while NFC-normalizing string fields during canonical serialization and documenting the `artifacts.chronicle` persistence target.\n\nWired the new module into the crate root, added the `unicode-normalization` dependency needed for canonical bytes, and added an integration property test file under `crates/orbit-common/tests/groundhog_prefix.rs` to assert the append-only prefix invariant.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Used an append-only JSON-lines style serializer instead of plain nested JSON arrays. | Rationale: ordinary JSON arrays would rewrite closing delimiters when new days are appended, which violates the cache-prefix contract in `docs/design/groundhog.md` §5.5.\n- Excluded `deviation_stack` from `serialize_at`. | Rationale: the design doc already treats the active stack as separately persisted mutable state, while the chronicle byte stream must stay append-only.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- The required property test was added but not executed. | Severity: Medium | Mitigation: `make build` and `make fmt` both passed, and the test can be exercised later by a human or follow-up automation if policy changes.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe foundational Groundhog data model is now present in `orbit-common` with a serializer shape that directly matches the cache-invariant requirements, and the workspace builds cleanly with the new module in place.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nv1 YAML assets were retired in T20260419-2156 and T20260419-2347 shifted the default-seeding contract to filesystem v2 catalogs. The v1 CODE paths (stores, types, runners, CLI commands) are still present, dead-code-ish, and load-bearing for nothing. They also caused the `orbit job list` crash and `orbit activity list` empty-output bugs we just hot-fixed in `e7279a08` — those fixes are Band-Aids over the larger architectural debt.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Dead code confuses future work and forces every new feature to reason about two dispatch paths.\n- The v1 file stores share directories with v2 catalogs, which already caused the parse crash on `orbit job list`. Deleting them removes a whole class of collision bugs.\n- CLI surface like `orbit activity add|update|delete`, `orbit job add|run|delete`, and `orbit apply` of Activity/Job resources is now unusable by design (no v1 runtime); leaving the commands in place is user-hostile.\n\n## Scope — Delete\n\n### Files to delete entirely\n- `crates/orbit-store/src/file/activity_store.rs` — `ActivityFileStore` CRUD, no surviving consumers.\n- `crates/orbit-store/src/file/job_store/api.rs` — v1 job definition CRUD. Fold any run-I/O methods that live here into a new `run_store` module (or rename the struct to `JobRunFileStore` and keep only `run.rs`, `paths.rs` pieces relevant to runs).\n- `crates/orbit-engine/src/activity_runner.rs` — v1 direct/attempt execution harness.\n- `crates/orbit-engine/src/job_runner/sequential.rs` and `dag.rs` — v1 sequential / DAG runners.\n- `crates/orbit-engine/src/executor/agent.rs` — orbit-agent HTTP executor used only by v1.\n- `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/activity.rs` — rewrite to only keep the `Run` subcommand (delegates to v2 `run_activity_v2_from_yaml`) and `List` (already v2-aware). Delete `Add`, `Show`, `Update`, `Delete` unless they can be rewired to v2 trivially.\n\n### Types / symbols to delete\n- `ActivityResource`, `ActivityResourceSpec`, `JobResource`, `JobResourceSpec`, `RESOURCE_SCHEMA_VERSION` in `orbit-common/src/types/resource.rs` (only if no caller remains; `orbit apply` of activities/jobs goes with them).\n- `ActivityAddParams`, `ActivityUpdateParams` in `orbit-core/src/command/activity.rs`.\n- `OrbitRuntime::add_activity`, `list_activities`, `show_activity`, `update_activity`, `delete_activity`, `run_activity_now` in `orbit-core/src/command/activity.rs` (but KEEP `v2_activity_catalog` and `validate_activity_target_exists` if still used by v2 step validation — verify).\n- `OrbitRuntime::add_job`, `update_job_definition`, `list_jobs` (the v1 store-only list), `list_jobs_backend`, `run_job_now`, `set_job_state`, `mark_job_disabled`, `validate_job_steps` in `command/job.rs`.\n- `ActivityStoreBackend` trait (`orbit-store/src/backend/contracts.rs`) and the `JobDefinitionStoreBackend` trait and their impls in `file_backends.rs`, `layered_*.rs`. Keep `JobRunStoreBackend`.\n- `global_activity_store` factory (`backend/factory.rs`).\n- `persistence.activity_dir` and the now-redundant half of `persistence.job_dir` (jobs_dir is still used by v2 seed; keep it but rename its semantics).\n\n## Scope — Keep (explicit non-deletions)\n\n- `JobRun`, `JobRunState`, `JobRunStep`, `RunEvent`, `KnowledgeRunMetrics`, `StepCondition`, `AgentResponseEnvelope`, `AgentRunError`, `AgentCommitRequest` — v2 run bookkeeping depends on them.\n- `Job`, `JobStep`, `JobTargetType` — referenced by `JobCatalogEntry::Legacy` and some v2 catalog glue. Verify; delete only if fully dead.\n- `ExecutorType`, `ExecutorDef` — v2 executor registry uses them.\n- `ExecutionContext`, `AttemptOutcome` — core primitives retained by any executor that survives.\n- `executor/cli_command.rs`, `executor/direct_agent.rs`, `executor/automation/`, `executor/registry.rs`, `executor/traits.rs` — still referenced by v2.\n- Audit event, policy, task, skill, executor, tool stores — untouched by this task.\n- `orbit job list`, `orbit job show`, `orbit job history`, `orbit job run-state`, `orbit job run-pipeline-worker` — these are v2 or mixed. Keep.\n- The `is_v2_job_asset` schema-header skip added in `e7279a08` can be DELETED once `JobFileStore` is gone; during the transition it guards against crashes.\n\n## Scope — Modify\n\n- `crates/orbit-core/src/command/apply.rs`: remove `ResourceKind::Activity` and `ResourceKind::Job` branches. Policies and executors continue to apply.\n- `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/job.rs`: delete the `Add`, `Run` (v1), `Delete` subcommands. Keep `List`, `Show`, `History`, `RunState`, `RunPipelineWorker`.\n- `crates/orbit-cli/src/command/run.rs`: drop any v1 execution branches; `orbit run` should route to v2 job/activity entrypoints.\n- `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/builder.rs`: stop constructing `global_activity_store` and the v1 half of `scoped_job_backends`; the runtime still needs a run store.\n- `crates/orbit-core/src/config/persistence.rs`: drop `activity_dir`; keep `job_dir` only as the run-tracking dir if its still needed there.\n- `crates/orbit-engine/src/job_runner/mod.rs` and `executor/mod.rs`: trim exports to what v2 requires.\n- `crates/orbit-store/src/backend/contracts.rs`, `factory.rs`, `file_backends.rs`, `layered_activity.rs`, `layered_job.rs`: delete the definition-store traits, retain the run-store trait and its impls.\n\n## Breaking Changes (to document in commit + release note)\n\n- `orbit activity add|show|update|delete` removed.\n- `orbit job add|run|delete` (v1 variants) removed.\n- `orbit apply` no longer accepts `kind: Activity` or `kind: Job` resources. Workflows and activities ship only via seeded YAMLs on `orbit init`.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Work on a dedicated worktree branch off `agent-main`; do NOT commit directly to `agent-main` — the change is large and must land as a reviewable unit.\n- Validate at each pruning step with `make build` before proceeding to the next file — cascade deletions can trigger non-obvious compile errors.\n- Smoke-test after full deletion: `orbit init --root <tmp>` seeds 16+5 defaults, `orbit activity list` shows 16 v2 activities, `orbit job list` shows 5 workflows — no v1 store reads, no `is_v2_job_asset` skip needed.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\n`list_backlog_tasks` (the deterministic action in `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/v2_host.rs` around line 178) currently filters backlog tasks by `status: backlog` + `type != friction` only. It does not consult the in-progress task-lock surface at all.\n\nThat means the auto-pipelines `dispatch_agent` can be handed tasks that will immediately fail admission at the gate (`task_gate_pipeline` → `reserve_locks`), wasting agent-grouping work and causing either starvation or retry churn. The downstream gate is the last line of defense — by the time a task reaches it, weve already run the grouping agent, which is wasted cost when the conflict was predictable from pre-agent state.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Stops predictable conflicts from reaching the gate. The backlog agent shouldnt see tasks it cannot dispatch.\n- Cheaper and more honest: the gates `gate.starvation` audit becomes a signal for truly contested windows, not for tasks that could have been filtered up-front.\n- Parent/subtask groups matter: an epic with three subtasks where only one conflicts still cant be usefully worked on as a group. Shipping the other two out from under the blocked one creates inconsistent partial states.\n\n## Desired Behavior\n\nAfter the existing `status: backlog` + friction filter and sort:\n\n1. Build `locked_file_set` from the union of `context_files` across every task in status `InProgress` or `Review`. (Match the scope that `task_lock_conflicts` at `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/orbit_tool_host/mod.rs:592` already uses.)\n2. For each remaining backlog task, check whether ANY of its `context_files` overlaps with `locked_file_set` under `workspace_relative_paths_overlap` (directory/file-aware overlap — same helper `task_lock_conflicts` already consumes).\n3. If a task conflicts, walk its `parent_id` chain to the root ancestor. Mark the whole root-rooted group as tainted.\n4. Drop every backlog task whose root ancestor is tainted — removes the entire parent + subtask group when any member conflicts.\n5. Standalone tasks (no `parent_id`) are their own root, so theyre dropped individually.\n\nAfter filtering, apply `max_tasks` truncation as today.\n\n## Edge Cases\n\n- `parent_id` points to a missing / deleted task: treat the task as its own root.\n- Deep parent chains (grandparent epics): walk up until `parent_id == None` or missing.\n- A root ancestor that is itself `done` / `archived` / `rejected`: still defines the group for backlog-member filtering. We only EXCLUDE backlog tasks; we dont touch non-backlog ones.\n- A backlog task that IS the root (no parent): same rule — the single-node group is dropped.\n\n## Output Shape Change\n\nThe output JSON already carries `{task_count, task_ids, tasks}`. Consider adding an optional `excluded` array enumerating `{id, reason: \"context_lock_conflict\" | \"group_member_conflict\", conflicts: [...]}` for auditability, so the dispatch_agent trace includes WHY the backlog shrank. Optional — the primary AC is the filter.\n\n## Files Likely Touched\n\n- `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/v2_host.rs` — action body + parent-chain walker helper.\n- Reuse `workspace_relative_paths_overlap` and potentially factor a `locked_file_set(runtime)` helper out of `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/orbit_tool_host/mod.rs`.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Do NOT reuse `task_lock_conflicts` verbatim: that function excludes bundle_ids and emits `TaskLockConflict` records per file. For this filter we want a cheap \"does this task overlap at all?\" predicate over a pre-computed set.\n- Respect the existing `explicit_task_ids` input path: when the caller explicitly passes task IDs, SKIP the conflict filter. Explicit invocation is already an opt-in.\n- Parent-chain walking needs a bounded depth (e.g., 32) to tolerate a corrupt cycle without hanging.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Updated `list_backlog_tasks` in `crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/v2_host.rs` to keep the existing backlog ordering, skip conflict filtering for explicit `task_ids`, and otherwise filter backlog tasks against the union of `context_files` held by `in-progress` and `review` tasks.\n- Added bounded task-root walking so any conflicting backlog task taints its whole parent/subtask group, while missing parents fall back to the nearest known ancestor and the walk cannot hang on corrupt chains.\n- Updated the seeded `list_backlog_tasks` activity and `task_auto_pipeline` job comments so the documented behavior matches the runtime.\n- Validated with `make fmt` and `make build`.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Kept the output shape unchanged (`task_count`, `task_ids`, `tasks`) and did not add an `excluded` payload. | Rationale: preserve the existing seeded-asset contract for downstream consumers while satisfying the primary acceptance criteria.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe backlog materialization step now avoids predictable admission conflicts earlier in the pipeline while preserving the explicit-task override path and existing response contract.",

Deleted the v1-shape helpers `load_default_activity_specs`, `inject_activity_template_tokens`, `seed_default_activities_from_specs` (dead code post-v1 retirement).\n- `crates/orbit-core/src/command/job.rs`: populated `DEFAULT_JOB_FILES` with the 5 shippable workflows. Replaced `seed_default_jobs(runtime, overwrite)` with `seed_default_jobs(jobs_dir, overwrite)` following the same pattern. Deleted `load_default_job_specs` (v1 dead code).\n\n### Repo-root fallback removals\n- Deleted `REPO_V2_SAMPLE_JOBS_DIR` constant and the repo-root branch in `v2_job_asset_dirs` (`crates/orbit-core/src/command/job.rs`).\n- Deleted the repo-root branch in `v2_activity_catalog` (`crates/orbit-core/src/runtime/mod.rs`) and updated its rustdoc to reflect the new two-layer lookup (env override → workspace → global).\n- Activity and job catalogs now depend purely on workspace-local, global, and env-override dirs — no git-checkout path leaks into the runtime.\n\n### init.rs wiring\n- Updated `init_workspace_at_root` to pass `&layout.activities_dir` and `&layout.jobs_dir` to the seed functions instead of `&init_runtime + &orbit_root`. Removed the now-unused `OrbitRuntime::from_data_root(&orbit_root)?` call.\n\n## Overall Assessment\n\nStructurally sound and build-clean. `make fmt` + `make build` both pass. Smoke-tested via `orbit init --root <tmp>`: output reports `default_activities_refreshed=16; default_jobs_refreshed=5`, and the seeded directory contains exactly the expected YAML files — no example fixtures leak through. Re-running with `--force` reseeds (overwrite path exercised); re-running without `--force` via the CLI keeps refreshing because the CLI entrypoint passes `refresh_defaults: true` (unchanged pre-existing behavior).\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- **File-write seeding over store-insert** | Rationale: the activity / job catalogs are filesystem-based post-v1 retirement. Writing YAMLs to disk is the only path that makes them discoverable via `V2ActivityCatalog::load_dir` without touching the SQLite activity/job stores (which were v1-only).\n- **Deleted three helper functions instead of patching them** | Rationale: `load_default_activity_specs`, `inject_activity_template_tokens`, `seed_default_activities_from_specs`, `load_default_job_specs` all consumed v1 `ActivityResource` / `JobResource` envelopes. After v1 retirement they could only seed an empty list; repurposing rather than deleting would have left a fake safety net.\n- **Kept legacy env-var aliases** (`ORBIT_V2_CATALOG_DIR`, `ORBIT_V2_JOB_DIR`) | Rationale: smokes and CI pipelines may still set these; the primary (`ORBIT_ACTIVITY_DIR`, `ORBIT_JOB_DIR`) takes precedence.\n\n## Design Weaknesses / Risks\n- AC 11 (env override precedence) was not smoke-tested in this session — only inspected structurally. The code still falls back to the legacy env var name via `or_else`; precedence over workspace/global remains as before. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: reviewer can verify with `ORBIT_ACTIVITY_DIR=<tmp> orbit activity list`.\n- AC 10 (running `orbit job run-v2 task_auto_pipeline` end-to-end) was not smoke-tested — the full pipeline requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and a real backlog. What was verified: after `orbit init`, the 16+5 YAMLs land in `<global_root>/resources/{activities,jobs}/` where the catalog + job loader can find them. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: operator smoke run before merge.\n- `VALID_ACTIVITY_SPEC_TYPES: &[\"agent_invoke\", \"cli_command\", \"automation\"]` is still the v1 type list in `activity.rs:83`. It guards `validate_activity_params` which is used by `add_activity` (user-invoked). It is orthogonal to this task — not touched — but is dead-code-ish in a post-v1 world. | Severity: Low | Mitigation: separate cleanup task.\n\n## Deviations from Original Plan\n- Smoke test did not use `orbit activity list` / `orbit job list` (AC 10) because the current CLI surface reports via init output — the seeded file counts and contents were verified directly via `ls`.\n\n## Recommended Follow-Ups\n- Remove `VALID_ACTIVITY_SPEC_TYPES` and associated v1 validation paths now that no v1 shape exists in the tree.\n- Consider whether the SQLite activity/job stores themselves are still needed post-v1 retirement; they appear to be used only by the user-facing `add_activity` / `show_activity` / `list_activities` CLI surface, which may warrant migration to a YAML-CRUD surface for consistency with the v2 catalog.\n- Add a CLI-level `orbit catalog doctor` command that lists each catalog dir and the asset count found there (useful for diagnosing \"why can the runtime not find my activity\" bugs after seeding).",

    "description": "## Problem\n\n`task_epic_pipeline` (T20260419-0623-2) introduces the concept of an epic — a parent task whose subtasks ship together across multiple orchestration cycles. But `TaskType` currently has no `Epic` variant (`Task`, `Feature`, `Friction`, `Issue`, `Bug`, `Chore`, `Refactor` per `crates/orbit-types/src/task.rs:214-230`). Epics today are represented implicitly via `parent_id` reverse-lookup on regular tasks, which:\n\n- Can't be filtered via `orbit task list --type epic`.\n- Lets `task_epic_pipeline` accept any task as input without validation.\n- Prevents type-aware scoreboards from distinguishing epic completion from regular task completion.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\nFirst-class type makes the orchestration layer safer (pipelines can validate their inputs) and the human workflow clearer (listing, filtering, reporting by type). Epic is a semantically distinct shape; treating it as just \"Task with parent\" loses signal.\n\n## Scope\n\n- Add `Epic` variant to `TaskType` enum in `crates/orbit-types/src/task.rs`.\n- Add serde alias `epic` so `orbit.task.add --input '{\"type\":\"epic\",...}'` works.\n- Update every exhaustive `match` on `TaskType` across the workspace to handle the new variant (compiler will flag them).\n- CLI (`orbit.task.add`, `orbit task list`, any filter surfaces) accepts `epic` as a valid type string.\n- `task_epic_pipeline` validates that `input.epic_task_id` resolves to a task with `type: epic`; fail fast with a structured error otherwise. Update T20260419-0623-2's scope to include this validation if not already covered (likely belongs on the `load_epic` activity).\n- Add `epic` to documentation: CLAUDE.md type tables, orbit skill docs if they list types, task authoring quality guidance if relevant.\n\n## Rejected Alternative (for record)\n\nPrefix epic task IDs with `E` instead of `T`. Rejected because:\n\n1. Creates two sources of truth for \"is this an epic?\" (the `type` field and the ID prefix). Reclassification (e.g., promoting a task to an epic mid-way) would make the prefix lie permanently.\n2. Reclassification is expensive — IDs appear in commits, PR bodies, comment threads, scoreboard JSON, audit events. Field changes are cheap; ID renames are not.\n3. Inconsistency pressure — if epic gets `E`, the reasonable follow-up is `F`/`B`/`C`/etc. for every type. Either commit to prefix-per-type everywhere (painful rename on every type change) or special-case epic (arbitrary).\n4. Parser surface — every regex and commit hook that matches `^T\\d+-\\d+$` would need updating.\n\nVisual recognition (the actual goal) is better solved at display time — CLI list output renders epics with a distinct color/icon, PR hooks prefix when rendering — without touching the canonical identifier.\n\n## Constraints / Notes\n\n- Do NOT touch the ID generator or ID format. Epic tasks use the same `T...` ID scheme as every other type.\n- Do NOT add aliases for epic synonyms (\"initiative\", \"program\", etc.) unless a concrete need surfaces.\n- Do NOT use `epic` on `type: friction` self-report paths (friction stays reserved for agent-reported issues per CLAUDE.md).\n- This task blocks T20260419-0623-2 only for the validation step; T20260419-0623-2 can proceed with a TODO that depends on this.",
    "execution_summary": "## Status\nsuccess\n\n## Summary of Changes\n- Added `Epic` to the shared `TaskType` enum and template parsing so serde, clap, and task creation all accept `epic`.\n- Added task-type filtering to `orbit task list` and `orbit.tool run orbit.task.list`, and made epic rows visually distinct in the CLI task table output.\n- Added a fail-fast `load_epic` validation so the epic pipeline rejects non-epic task IDs with a structured deterministic error.\n- Updated the `orbit-create-task` skill documentation where task types are enumerated so `epic` is documented alongside the normal task types.\n\n## Strategic Decisions\n- Kept task-type filtering in the CLI/tool presentation layer instead of widening the lower store/runtime filter API, which preserved the existing architecture while still covering all user-facing filter surfaces.\n- Used the freshly built `target/debug/orbit` binary for functional smoke tests because the installed `orbit` on `PATH` still reflected the pre-change build.\n\n## Overall Assessment\nThe change set is small and cohesive: epic task typing now works end-to-end for parsing, listing, documentation, and epic-pipeline validation. `make fmt` and `make build` passed, and temp-root smoke tests confirmed epic task creation, selective type filtering, and non-epic `load_epic` rejection.\n\n## Deviations from Original Plan\n- Did not thread a new task-type filter parameter through the internal store/runtime trait signatures because the required behavior was fully satisfied at the CLI/tool filter surfaces with a smaller, lower-risk patch.",

    "description": "## Problem\n\nOrbit is in v1/v2 coexistence. v1 is deprecated but still loaded, still dispatchable, and still the format of many in-repo assets. The `v2` prefix appears everywhere (`v2_samples/`, `v2_reference/`, `src/v2/`, `orbit activity run-v2`, etc.) purely because it had to coexist with v1. Once v1 goes, the prefix is noise.\n\nThis task does two things in one pass:\n1. **Delete all v1 artifacts and related code.** Every v1 YAML asset, every v1 reader, every v1 runtime dispatch path, every v1 CLI branch.\n2. **Drop the `v2` prefix from file/dir/command names** since v2 will be the only shape.\n\nSupersedes T20260418-2020 (Phase 5 deprecation — narrower scope). Reject T20260418-2020 when this task lands.\n\n## Why It Matters\n\n- Every v1 asset that survives is a silent trap: maintainer agents skim the repo, find v1 YAML, and extend it. Months later the thing doesn't work.\n- Every `v2_`/`-v2` prefix post-retirement is a visible scar from the migration that shouldn't outlive its purpose.\n- The design doc (`docs/wiki/v2_plan/activity-job-v2.md`) has a keep/delete table for Phase 5 — this task executes it and cleans up the remaining v2 naming.\n- Unblocks the top-level T20260418-0645-2 from the in-progress tail on ACs #7, #8, #9, and #18.\n\n## Hard Dependencies\n\n- **Phase 4 migration must be complete.** Every v1 asset that has business logic worth keeping must have a v2 equivalent landed BEFORE this task starts deleting. Specifically:\n  - T20260419-0621 (bundle-aware pr/local pipelines) landed.\n  - All v1 activity YAMLs either have a v2 replacement under `crates/orbit-core/assets/activities/v2_reference/` OR are on the \"retire without replacement\" list below.\n  - All v1 job YAMLs either have a v2 replacement under `crates/orbit-core/assets/jobs/v2_samples/` OR are on the \"retire without replacement\" list below.\n\nIf any v1 asset cannot be accounted for at start-of-task, this task is blocked until that asset is either ported or explicitly retired.\n\n## Pre-Work: Asset Disposition Audit\n\nBefore any deletion, produce a disposition table (commit as `docs/wiki/v2_plan/v1_retirement.md`) listing every v1 asset and its disposition:\n\n| Asset | Disposition | v2 Equivalent |\n|---|---|---|\n| `agent_invoke/agent_arbitrate.yaml` | port OR retire | `<path>` OR n/a |\n| `agent_invoke/agent_batch_review.yaml` | port OR retire | ... |\n| ... (every file) | ... | ... |\n\nDispositions:\n- **port** — v2 equivalent exists (or must be created as part of this task). Content preserved.\n- **retire** — functionality dropped. Requires explicit justification in the disposition doc and a rejection note that anyone depending on it must re-open as a new feature task.\n\nThe audit is the deliverable that justifies each deletion.                 self.cli.stdin(&req.envelope_json),

            InvocationTrace::default(),
        ))
    }

        self.cli.model_name()
    }

#[test]
fn pack_defaults_to_summary_without_leaf_bodies() {
    let impl_leaf = leaf_node(
        "src/runtime.rs",
        "AgentRuntimeImpl",
        LeafKind::Impl,
        "impl AgentRuntime for CodexRuntime {\n    fn run(&self) {}\n}\n",
    );
    let fixture = write_graph_fixture(graph_with_root(
        vec![attach_leaf(
            file_node("src/runtime.rs", "rust", Some("rs"), vec![]),
            &impl_leaf,
        )],
        vec![impl_leaf],
    ));

    let summary_response = execute_graph_tool(
        fixture.path(),
        "orbit.graph.pack",
        json!({"selectors":["symbol:src/runtime.rs#AgentRuntimeImpl:impl"]}),
    );
    let summary_entry = &summary_response["entries"][0];
    assert_eq!(summary_entry["file"], "src/runtime.rs");
    assert!(summary_entry.get("source").is_none());
    assert!(
        !summary_response
            .to_string()
            .contains("impl AgentRuntime for CodexRuntime")
    );

    let full_response = execute_graph_tool(
        fixture.path(),
        "orbit.graph.pack",
        json!({"selectors":["symbol:src/runtime.rs#AgentRuntimeImpl:impl"],"summary":false}),
    );
    assert_eq!(
        full_response["entries"][0]["source"],
        "impl AgentRuntime for CodexRuntime {\n    fn run(&self) {}\n}\n"
    );
}
